EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper-level trough dove south and closed off into a low over Arizona during the day of April 4th. Lift ahead of the low along with a backdoor front diving south and west across eastern New Mexico and into the Rio Grande Valley resulted in the development of snow across eastern, northern and west central New Mexico late April 4th into April 5th. Snow peaked in intensity, especially along the east slopes of the central mountain chain during the morning hours of April 5th due to upslope flow behind the backdoor front along with the upper low approaching the bootheel in southwest New Mexico. Snow and blowing snow resulted in the closure of U.S. Highway 64/87 in far northeast NM for several hours as well as sections of I-40 from Tijeras Canyon to Santa Rosa due to several vehicle accidents.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The SNOTEL site at Taos Pueblo observed an estimated 10 inches of snowfall.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An unseasonably and record deep upper trough for mid-March moved through the intermountain West late on March 13th closing off into a deep upper low on March 14th. Strong south to southwest winds ahead of the trough resulted in blowing dust across southern Chaves County resulting in the closure of NM Highway 2 at Hagerman. The Pacific front moved through the state during the overnight hours of March 13th into March 14th. Subsidence behind the Pacific front combined with strong mid and upper-level winds at the base of this trough, a strong pressure gradient south and west of the newly formed upper low and 975 mb surface low across the central Great Plains resulted in widespread high wind gusts of 60 to 80 mph across central New Mexico from the early morning to around midday and across eastern New Mexico during the morning through mid afternoon hours. These wind gusts behind the Pacific front resulted in wind damage across several zones in eastern New Mexico along with widespread blowing dust across the Lower Rio Grande Valley and eastern New Mexico. Strong and dangerous westerly crosswinds even closed down I-25 from Las Vegas to Raton during the late morning to mid afternoon hours. Winds and blowing dust subsided during the late afternoon and early evening hours as the strong storm system and associated higher mid and upper-level winds, exited east of the state.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Roswell Portable 2 measured a peak wind gust of 58 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The combination of very dry antecedent ground conditions and very dry and strong southwesterly flow ahead of a closed upper low over the Great Basin region brought widespread high wind gusts and blowing dust across mainly central and eastern NM during the afternoon and early evening of April 27th. Peak wind gusts of 58 to 80 mph were observed across the area. Blowing dust causing near zero visibility resulted in the closure of the Roswell Relief Route, as well as the closure of NM Highway 2 at Hagerman, NM. The high winds and low humidity resulted in extreme critical fire weather conditions. Unfortunately two fires broke out, the Otero and Balsam Fires near Socorro and Grants, NM respectively. The Balsam Fire destroyed 6 structures and 8 outbuildings. Conditions improved during the evening as winds began to subside.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Raton Crews Municipal Airport measured a peak wind gust of 62 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An intense trough digging into the Four Corners region with rapid cyclogenesis over eastern Colorado produced high winds and blowing dust across much of the southern Great Plains.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Eddy County local law enforcement reported at least 13 vehicle accidents, mainly on US Highway 25. Approximately 30 to 40 vehicles were involved in all accidents. Injuries were reported, but no further information was provided. Blowing dust along US Highway 285 caused by high winds between 60 and 80 MPH reduced visibility to near zero for several hours. The time and amount of property damage are estimates for all events.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: As a strengthening low pressure system continued to move into the Upper Midwest, strong west winds overspread much of the southern Great Plains. A strong westerly jet streak behind this developing weather system aided these winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Westerly synoptic winds at the West Texas Mesonet site, Carlsbad Caverns NP, resulted in a maximum wind gust of 70 MPH.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A quickly strengthening area of surface low pressure over eastern Colorado ahead of a potent trough pushed a strong front through the region. During the evening and early overnight hours, high winds behind this front were recorded across southeast New Mexico and the higher elevations of West Texas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A maximum wind gust of 61 MPH was recorded at the CWOP mesonet station FW9687 Eunice.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A compact shortwave trough entering the southern Great Plains resulted in westerly high winds across portions of West Texas and Southeast New Mexico's higher elevations and adjacent plains.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A Facebook photo shows a fallen tree due to high winds in Carlsbad. The tree fell between about 9:30 AM MST and 11:55 AM MST, when the report was received. Wind speeds were estimated to be approximately 60 MPH.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A quickly strengthening area of surface low pressure over eastern Colorado ahead of a potent trough pushed a strong front through the region. During the evening and early overnight hours, high winds behind this front were recorded across southeast New Mexico and the higher elevations of West Texas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A maximum wind gust of 70 mph was recorded at the West Texas Mesonet site, Carlsbad Caverns NP.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A maximum wind gust of 58 MPH was recorded at the West Texas Mesonet site, Carlsbad Caverns NP.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A compact shortwave trough entering the southern Great Plains resulted in westerly high winds across portions of West Texas and Southeast New Mexico's higher elevations and adjacent plains.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A maximum wind gust of 62 MPH was reported by the CWOP mesonet station FW9690 Jal.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A broad long-wave trough with progressive jet stream resulted in high winds across the Guadalupe, Delaware, and Davis Mountains and immediately adjacent plains.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 74 MPH wind gust was measured at the Carlsbad Caverns West Texas Mesonet site in New Mexico.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper-level trough with an intense westerly jet streak at its base produced high winds across the Guadalupe and Delaware Mountains and near Carlsbad Caverns National Park.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: West Texas Mesonet station Carlsbad Caverns National Park reported a maximum wind gust of 68 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Little to no precipitation fell in February which lead to many areas seeing worsening drought conditions in southern New Mexico.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The month started out with the western half of the zone in Severe(D2) drought and the east in Extreme(D3) drought.  By the end of the month, the entire zone was in Extreme(D3) drought.  There was no precipitation at the Antelope Wells Cooperative Observer which was 0.43 inches below normal.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Little to no precipitation fell in February which lead to many areas seeing worsening drought conditions in southern New Mexico.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The entire zone went from Severe(D2) drought to Extreme(D3) drought during the month of February.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Little to no precipitation fell in February which lead to many areas seeing worsening drought conditions in southern New Mexico.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The zone started out with the southern portion of the zone in Extreme(D3) drought improving to Moderate(D1) drought over the north.  By the end of February, all of the zone was in Extreme(D3) drought except the far northeast where it was placed in Severe(D2) drought.  The Deming airport received only a trace of precipitation which was 0.62 inches below normal.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Little to no precipitation fell in February which lead to many areas seeing worsening drought conditions in southern New Mexico.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The southern half of the zone started in Severe(D2) drought while the north was in Moderate(D1) drought.  By the end of the month, most of the west was in Extreme(D3) drought with the remainder of the zone in Severe(D2) drought.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Little to no precipitation fell in February which lead to many areas seeing worsening drought conditions in southern New Mexico.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Most of the zone remained in Extreme(D3) drought with Severe(D2) drought mainly in the northeastern portion of the zone. There was no precipitation at the Cooperative Observer at New Mexico State University which was 0.36 inches below normal.

CZ_NAME: WEST SLOPES SACRAMENTO MOUNTAINS BELOW 7500 FEET
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Little to no precipitation fell in February which lead to many areas seeing worsening drought conditions in southern New Mexico.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Extreme(D3) drought continued over the far southeast portion of the zone while areas across the north transitioned from Moderate(D1) drought to mainly Severe(D2) drought. The Mountain Park Cooperative Observer reported 0.11 inches of precipitation which was 0.95 inches below normal.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A broad upper trough was moving out of the Great Basin and into the Four Corners region.  Ahead of this trough, strong southwest winds picked up across Luna county with gusts over 50 mph reported resulting in numerous road closures.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Winds gusted up to 55 mph at the Deming airport resulting in widespread blowing dust across Luna county.  The NMDOT needed to close Interstate 10 east of Deming due to the visibilities dropping to near zero.  In addition to the interstate closure, Highway 11 south of Deming was closed also due to limited visibility.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A back door cold front pushed through southern New Mexico the morning of February 27th. On the back side of the front, east to southeast winds increased across the Lordsburg Playa and created dust channels which reduced visibilities to near zero along Interstate 10 resulting in a fatal accident.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Winds gusted over 50 mph at times along the Lordsburg Playa resulting in dust channels that created near zero visibilities.  These visibilities affected Interstate 10 and caused an 11 vehicle accident around mile marker 10.  The accident and continued reduced visibilities at times kept the interstate closed through much of the day.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A back door cold front pushed west across southern New Mexico and as it reached the Lordsburg Playa, a dust storm developed and caused a multiple vehicle accident on Interstate 10.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A multiple vehicle crash was caused by blowing dust and low visibility.  Injuries were reported but number is unknown.  This happened around mile marker 9 on Interstate 10 west of Lordsburg.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An unseasonably and record deep upper trough for mid-March moved through the intermountain West late on March 13th closing off into a deep upper low on March 14th. Strong south to southwest winds ahead of the trough resulted in blowing dust across southern Chaves County resulting in the closure of NM Highway 2 at Hagerman. The Pacific front moved through the state during the overnight hours of March 13th into March 14th. Subsidence behind the Pacific front combined with strong mid and upper-level winds at the base of this trough, a strong pressure gradient south and west of the newly formed upper low and 975 mb surface low across the central Great Plains resulted in widespread high wind gusts of 60 to 80 mph across central New Mexico from the early morning to around midday and across eastern New Mexico during the morning through mid afternoon hours. These wind gusts behind the Pacific front resulted in wind damage across several zones in eastern New Mexico along with widespread blowing dust across the Lower Rio Grande Valley and eastern New Mexico. Strong and dangerous westerly crosswinds even closed down I-25 from Las Vegas to Raton during the late morning to mid afternoon hours. Winds and blowing dust subsided during the late afternoon and early evening hours as the strong storm system and associated higher mid and upper-level winds, exited east of the state.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Roswell Relief Route from Sunset Avenue to Brasher Road, U.S. 285 from mile marker 105 to mile marker 110, and State Road 2 from mile marker 17, Hagerman to mile marker 21, 1 mile north of Greenfield due to near zero visibility from blowing dust. Visibility at the Roswell Air Center dropped blow a quarter of a mile due to blowing dust.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Overall drought conditions continued to worsen and expand across northern and central NM due to above normal temperatures and well below normal precipitation. Several storms brought high winds, high fire danger and widespread blowing dust due to the antecedent dry conditions in place. The high winds from these storms also helped play a role in worsening drought conditions across the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Extreme drought conditions were expanded to include the entire zone.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Another unseasonably deep upper trough moved into the western U.S. March 17th strengthening into a closed upper low over Colorado on March 18th. Strong south to southwest winds ahead of the trough resulted in blowing dust across southern Chaves County resulting in the closure of the Roswell Relief route on the southwest side of town during the afternoon of March 17th. High wind gusts of 60 to 80 mph began across the high terrain during the morning hours of March 18th moving down to lower elevations late morning as the Pacific front moved through the state. The subsidence behind the front helped to mix down strong mid and upper-level winds at the base of the newly developed low down to the surface. A strong pressure gradient was also present across the state south of a 990 mb surface low over eastern Colorado. These high wind gusts resulted in downed trees at Corrales in the Albuquerque Metro and in southwest Chaves County and blew a roof off a workshop near San Mateo in the west central mountains. The high winds also resulted in widespread blowing dust and near zero visibility across the Rio Grande Valley and eastern NM. The near zero visibility from the blowing dust resulted in multi-vehicle crashes on I-25 between Albuquerque and Santa Fe, I-40 in Torrance and Guadalupe County, and on U.S. Highway 285 south of Roswell. U.S. Highway 54 in the Carrizozo area was shut down for several hours due to the near zero visibility from the blowing dust. High winds from a shower induced microburst resulted in some power outages in east central Albuquerque during the mid evening. Winds and blowing dust subsided during the late evening hours across central and southern New Mexico, but continued across far northeast New Mexico behind a backdoor front until the late morning hours of March 19th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Clines Corners ASOS station measured a peak wind gust of 76 mph, as well as a high sustained wind speed of 51 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level low crossing the western CONUS brought a strong jetstream and Pacific cold front across New Mexico March 3rd. Strong southwesterly winds mixed down to the surface through much of the southeastern half of the state, producing gusts of 45 to 75 mph. Blowing dust was lofted up producing low to near zero visibility in parts of Chaves and Lincoln Counties. Two multi-vehicles crashes were reported along U.S. Highway 285, one near Lake Arthur and the other involving two semi-trucks just south of Roswell. Thankfully, no loss of life was reported. Strong northerly to northwesterly winds wrapped around the backside of the upper low as it exited east of NM during the night of March 3rd through the 4th. Winter weather in the form of blowing snow impacted areas from Raton Pass to Union County before high winds filled in across eastern NM. Strong west to northwest winds gusting 60 to 80 mph were observed over areas mainly along and east of the central mountain chain. High wind damage and blowing dust produced more impacts to property and closed area highways, including area highways near and south of Roswell, NM.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: High wind gusts of up to 61 mph observed across the area lofted blowing dust producing brownout and near zero visibility conditions. The NMDOT and local law enforcement closed portions of U.S. Highway 285 between Roswell and the Eddy County line, as well as the Roswell Relief Route southwest of town. Thankfully, there were no reports of car accidents.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: January 2025 was a colder than average month with some beneficial snow across central and southern NM from January 8th and 9th, north central NM during the middle of the month and across most of northern NM on January 29th and 30th. Despite this, snowpack remained well below the 30 year normal across the mountain ranges with some SNOTEL sites showing historically low snow water equivalent values. As a result, severe drought conditions across far southeastern areas slightly expanded with areas of severe drought added to northern areas of the northwest plateau, far northwest highlands, Tusa Mountains, much of the southwest mountains, and all of the San Francisco River Valley. An area of extreme drought was added to the southern portion of the San Francisco River Valley, including Glenwood.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Severe drought conditions were introduced to the northwest portion of the zone.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: High winds and elevated fire weather conditions resulted in a downed utility line on private land in Mora County east of I-25 and south of Wagon Mound that resulted in a fast moving wildfire. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: February 2025 was a well above normal warm and dry month across New Mexico. The western two-thirds of the state saw temperature anomalies in the warmest 10 percent on record with some spots seeing their warmest February on record. This, alongside roughly two-thirds of the state see little to no precipitation exacerbated the return of moderate and severe drought across much of the western two-thirds of NM. Extreme drought was introduced into the far southwestern corner of Catron County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Severe drought was introduced to the southern and eastern third of the zone.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An unseasonably and record deep upper trough for mid-March moved through the intermountain West late on March 13th closing off into a deep upper low on March 14th. Strong south to southwest winds ahead of the trough resulted in blowing dust across southern Chaves County resulting in the closure of NM Highway 2 at Hagerman. The Pacific front moved through the state during the overnight hours of March 13th into March 14th. Subsidence behind the Pacific front combined with strong mid and upper-level winds at the base of this trough, a strong pressure gradient south and west of the newly formed upper low and 975 mb surface low across the central Great Plains resulted in widespread high wind gusts of 60 to 80 mph across central New Mexico from the early morning to around midday and across eastern New Mexico during the morning through mid afternoon hours. These wind gusts behind the Pacific front resulted in wind damage across several zones in eastern New Mexico along with widespread blowing dust across the Lower Rio Grande Valley and eastern New Mexico. Strong and dangerous westerly crosswinds even closed down I-25 from Las Vegas to Raton during the late morning to mid afternoon hours. Winds and blowing dust subsided during the late afternoon and early evening hours as the strong storm system and associated higher mid and upper-level winds, exited east of the state.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy blowing dust resulted in near zero visibility on I-25 from milepost 166 to milepost 167 north of Socorro.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deep upper trough developed over the Great Basin during the day on March 6th with strong mid and upper-level winds ahead of this low and over New Mexico. The upper low also induced the development of a strong 989 mb surface lee low over southeast Colorado by the afternoon. The surface gradient south of this low along with deep daytime mixing resulted in high wind gusts of 60 to 75 mph across central and eastern New Mexico from midday through the evening hours. These strong southwest to west winds combined with the dry soil conditions resulted in blowing dust resulting in near zero visibility in Albuquerque and Roswell. High winds tapered off around sunset for most areas and around midnight across the northeast highlands.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Taos Ski Valley Kachina Peak station saw a peak gust of 78 mph, while their Highline Ridge station also saw a gust to 60 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level low crossing the western CONUS brought a strong jetstream and Pacific cold front across New Mexico March 3rd. Strong southwesterly winds mixed down to the surface through much of the southeastern half of the state, producing gusts of 45 to 75 mph. Blowing dust was lofted up producing low to near zero visibility in parts of Chaves and Lincoln Counties. Two multi-vehicles crashes were reported along U.S. Highway 285, one near Lake Arthur and the other involving two semi-trucks just south of Roswell. Thankfully, no loss of life was reported. Strong northerly to northwesterly winds wrapped around the backside of the upper low as it exited east of NM during the night of March 3rd through the 4th. Winter weather in the form of blowing snow impacted areas from Raton Pass to Union County before high winds filled in across eastern NM. Strong west to northwest winds gusting 60 to 80 mph were observed over areas mainly along and east of the central mountain chain. High wind damage and blowing dust produced more impacts to property and closed area highways, including area highways near and south of Roswell, NM.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Estancia FW3653 CWOP mesonet station measured a peak wind gust of 61 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper low was moving through the Great Basin with a 150 knot jet entering into western New Mexico.  A deep surface low over eastern Colorado brought a tight pressure gradient across southern New Mexico bringing wind gusts to 80 mph along with near zero visibilities with blowing dust.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Salinas Peak Mesonet site reported a peak gust of 80 mph.  Some other gusts across the zone include 76 mph at San Augustin Pass, 66 mph at the White Sands Missile Range Building 1830 and 61 mph at the Globe Mesonet site.

CZ_NAME: EAST SLOPES SACRAMENTO MOUNTAINS BELOW 7500 FEET
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper trough extended southwest through the Four Corners into the Baja region with a 140 knot jet moving just south of the New Mexico border.  This trough combined with a deep surface low over southeastern Colorado brought wind gusts up to 90 mph to the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A peak gust of 61 mph was recorded at the Lincoln Portable Number 2 RAWS north of Mayhill.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: February 2025 was a well above normal warm and dry month across New Mexico. The western two-thirds of the state saw temperature anomalies in the warmest 10 percent on record with some spots seeing their warmest February on record. This, alongside roughly two-thirds of the state see little to no precipitation exacerbated the return of moderate and severe drought across much of the western two-thirds of NM. Extreme drought was introduced into the far southwestern corner of Catron County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Severe and extreme drought was expanded to the rest of the zone.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: January 2025 was a colder than average month with some beneficial snow across central and southern NM from January 8th and 9th, north central NM during the middle of the month and across most of northern NM on January 29th and 30th. Despite this, snowpack remained well below the 30 year normal across the mountain ranges with some SNOTEL sites showing historically low snow water equivalent values. As a result, severe drought conditions across far southeastern areas slightly expanded with areas of severe drought added to northern areas of the northwest plateau, far northwest highlands, Tusa Mountains, much of the southwest mountains, and all of the San Francisco River Valley. An area of extreme drought was added to the southern portion of the San Francisco River Valley, including Glenwood.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Extreme drought conditions were introduced to the southern half portion of the zone.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper-level trough dove south and closed off into a low over Arizona during the day of April 4th. Lift ahead of the low along with a backdoor front diving south and west across eastern New Mexico and into the Rio Grande Valley resulted in the development of snow across eastern, northern and west central New Mexico late April 4th into April 5th. Snow peaked in intensity, especially along the east slopes of the central mountain chain during the morning hours of April 5th due to upslope flow behind the backdoor front along with the upper low approaching the bootheel in southwest New Mexico. Snow waned slowly from north to south across central and eastern New Mexico during afternoon hours as the low moved across the southern portion of the state into West Texas. Overall, snowfall amounts of 10 to 18 inches were observed across the east slopes of the Sangre de Cristo Mountains and Sandia and Manzano Mountains, around 5 to 10 inches across the northern mountains, and 4 to 8 inches across the west central highlands and northeast and east central highlands and plains. Snow and blowing snow resulted in the closure of U.S. Highway 64/87 in far northeast NM for several hours as well as sections of I-40 from Tijeras Canyon to Santa Rosa due to several vehicle accidents.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter in San Fidel measured 4.3 inches of snowfall. Elsewhere in the zone, a CoCoRaHS observer in Grants measured 4.2 inches of snowfall.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level low crossing the western CONUS brought a strong jetstream and Pacific cold front across New Mexico March 3rd. Strong southwesterly winds mixed down to the surface through much of the southeastern half of the state, producing gusts of 45 to 75 mph. Blowing dust was lofted up producing low to near zero visibility in parts of Chaves and Lincoln Counties. Two multi-vehicles crashes were reported along U.S. Highway 285, one near Lake Arthur and the other involving two semi-trucks just south of Roswell. Thankfully, no loss of life was reported. Strong northerly to northwesterly winds wrapped around the backside of the upper low as it exited east of NM during the night of March 3rd through the 4th. Winter weather in the form of blowing snow impacted areas from Raton Pass to Union County before high winds filled in across eastern NM. Strong west to northwest winds gusting 60 to 80 mph were observed over areas mainly along and east of the central mountain chain. High wind damage and blowing dust produced more impacts to property and closed area highways, including area highways near and south of Roswell, NM.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Nogal mesonet measured a peak gust of 64 mph. A peak gust of 60 mph was also measured at the Sierra Blanca Airport.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter in Wagon Mound measured 10 inches of snowfall. Slick roads from snow resulted in severe driving conditions on I-25 in the zone.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A trailing upper level disturbance moved through northern and central New Mexico on May 19th. Daytime heating resulted in the development of isolated to scattered virga showers and thunderstorms across central NM mid to late morning. This virga and shower activity resulted in high wind gusts across the central highlands, western Guadalupe County and Fort Sumner during the early to mid afternoon hours. Additionally, strong wind gusts from showers in the Albuquerque Metro resulted in down trees and tree branches in Corrales. Finally, a high wind gust of 60 mph was observed at Tucumcari Municipal Airport. Shower and storm activity exited east into Texas during the evening hours.||Key Words: Virga, High Wind Gusts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong wind gusts from showers resulted in a down tree on the north end of Corrales Road near Mountain View Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Another unseasonably deep upper trough moved into the western U.S. March 17th strengthening into a closed upper low over Colorado on March 18th. Strong south to southwest winds ahead of the trough resulted in blowing dust across southern Chaves County resulting in the closure of the Roswell Relief route on the southwest side of town during the afternoon of March 17th. High wind gusts of 60 to 80 mph began across the high terrain during the morning hours of March 18th moving down to lower elevations late morning as the Pacific front moved through the state. The subsidence behind the front helped to mix down strong mid and upper-level winds at the base of the newly developed low down to the surface. A strong pressure gradient was also present across the state south of a 990 mb surface low over eastern Colorado. These high wind gusts resulted in downed trees at Corrales in the Albuquerque Metro and in southwest Chaves County and blew a roof off a workshop near San Mateo in the west central mountains. The high winds also resulted in widespread blowing dust and near zero visibility across the Rio Grande Valley and eastern NM. The near zero visibility from the blowing dust resulted in multi-vehicle crashes on I-25 between Albuquerque and Santa Fe, I-40 in Torrance and Guadalupe County, and on U.S. Highway 285 south of Roswell. U.S. Highway 54 in the Carrizozo area was shut down for several hours due to the near zero visibility from the blowing dust. High winds from a shower induced microburst resulted in some power outages in east central Albuquerque during the mid evening. Winds and blowing dust subsided during the late evening hours across central and southern New Mexico, but continued across far northeast New Mexico behind a backdoor front until the late morning hours of March 19th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Farmington Airport ASOS measured a peak wind gust of 60 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An unseasonably and record deep upper trough for mid-March moved through the intermountain West late on March 13th closing off into a deep upper low on March 14th. Strong south to southwest winds ahead of the trough resulted in blowing dust across southern Chaves County resulting in the closure of NM Highway 2 at Hagerman. The Pacific front moved through the state during the overnight hours of March 13th into March 14th. Subsidence behind the Pacific front combined with strong mid and upper-level winds at the base of this trough, a strong pressure gradient south and west of the newly formed upper low and 975 mb surface low across the central Great Plains resulted in widespread high wind gusts of 60 to 80 mph across central New Mexico from the early morning to around midday and across eastern New Mexico during the morning through mid afternoon hours. These wind gusts behind the Pacific front resulted in wind damage across several zones in eastern New Mexico along with widespread blowing dust across the Lower Rio Grande Valley and eastern New Mexico. Strong and dangerous westerly crosswinds even closed down I-25 from Las Vegas to Raton during the late morning to mid afternoon hours. Winds and blowing dust subsided during the late afternoon and early evening hours as the strong storm system and associated higher mid and upper-level winds, exited east of the state.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Amistad mesonet station measured a peak gust that surpassed the instrumentation's peak gust limit of 67 mph. The Clayton ASOS also measured a peak gust of 66 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Abundant moisture pushed east to west across NM behind a strong cold front ahead of a strong upper level low approaching from AZ. The abundant moisture and favorable weather setup allowed for multiple days of strong to severe thunderstorms to develop across northern and central NM from May 4th through the 6th. Alongside all of this was unseasonably cold weather at higher elevations of the northern mountains. The Sangre de Cristo Mountains received 1 to 3 feet of snow at higher elevations above 9,500 feet. These high snow amounts ended up melting fairly quickly or succumbed to rain on snow events producing flooding impacts over portions of the east slopes of the Sangre de Cristo Mountains that lasted into May 8th.||Key Words: Heavy Snow, Severe Thunderstorms, River Flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Penny to quarter size hail in the East Mountain Communities.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deep upper trough developed over the Great Basin during the day on March 6th with strong mid and upper-level winds ahead of this low and over New Mexico. The upper low also induced the development of a strong 989 mb surface lee low over southeast Colorado by the afternoon. The surface gradient south of this low along with deep daytime mixing resulted in high wind gusts of 60 to 75 mph across central and eastern New Mexico from midday through the evening hours. These strong southwest to west winds combined with the dry soil conditions resulted in blowing dust resulting in near zero visibility in Albuquerque and Roswell. High winds tapered off around sunset for most areas and around midnight across the northeast highlands.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Cedar Drive NMCONCH1 public weather station measured a high sustained wind speed of 47 mph.

Alongside all of this was unseasonably cold weather at higher elevations of the northern mountains. The Sangre de Cristo Mountains received 1 to 3 feet of snow at higher elevations above 9,500 feet. These high snow amounts ended up melting fairly quickly or succumbed to rain on snow events producing flooding impacts over portions of the east slopes of the Sangre de Cristo Mountains that lasted into May 8th.||Key Words: Heavy Snow, Severe Thunderstorms, River Flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Continuing the pattern from May 24th and 25th, an incoming trough (with embedded disturbances) interacted with a dryline and produced isolated storms in the eastern Permian Basin of West Texas. One thunderstorm developed in Glasscock County and produced hail up to 2 inches in diameter south of Garden City before it moved east of the Midland/Odessa CWA. An additional thunderstorm moved into northern Lea County later in the evening and produced a 58 mph wind gust.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The West Texas Mesonet station 2 miles west/southwest of Tatum reported a thunderstorm wind gust of 58 mph.

CZ_NAME: MIDDLE RIO GRANDE VALLEY/ALBUQUERQUE METRO AREA
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deep upper trough developed over the Great Basin during the day on March 6th with strong mid and upper-level winds ahead of this low and over New Mexico. The upper low also induced the development of a strong 989 mb surface lee low over southeast Colorado by the afternoon. The surface gradient south of this low along with deep daytime mixing resulted in high wind gusts of 60 to 75 mph across central and eastern New Mexico from midday through the evening hours. These strong southwest to west winds combined with the dry soil conditions resulted in blowing dust resulting in near zero visibility in Albuquerque and Roswell. High winds tapered off around sunset for most areas and around midnight across the northeast highlands.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: NWS meteorologists observed low visibility under a quarter statute mile at the forecast office and the Albuquerque Sunport resulting from blowing dust.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An unseasonably and record deep upper trough for mid-March moved through the intermountain West late on March 13th closing off into a deep upper low on March 14th. Strong south to southwest winds ahead of the trough resulted in blowing dust across southern Chaves County resulting in the closure of NM Highway 2 at Hagerman. The Pacific front moved through the state during the overnight hours of March 13th into March 14th. Subsidence behind the Pacific front combined with strong mid and upper-level winds at the base of this trough, a strong pressure gradient south and west of the newly formed upper low and 975 mb surface low across the central Great Plains resulted in widespread high wind gusts of 60 to 80 mph across central New Mexico from the early morning to around midday and across eastern New Mexico during the morning through mid afternoon hours. These wind gusts behind the Pacific front resulted in wind damage across several zones in eastern New Mexico along with widespread blowing dust across the Lower Rio Grande Valley and eastern New Mexico. Strong and dangerous westerly crosswinds even closed down I-25 from Las Vegas to Raton during the late morning to mid afternoon hours. Winds and blowing dust subsided during the late afternoon and early evening hours as the strong storm system and associated higher mid and upper-level winds, exited east of the state.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Sandia Airpark public weather station measured a peak wind gust of 67 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: April saw below normal precipitation across much of the state, resulting in a significant expansion of Extreme Drought conditions, mainly along and west of the Rio Grande Valley. The below normal precipitation combined with several strong wind events allowed for the worsening drought conditions.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Severe drought persisted southern and western portions of the zone while being removed from the northeastern portion of the zone.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front backed through northeastern NM underlying westerly flow aloft. Severe thunderstorms developed behind the front over the northeastern plains of NM toward the TX line that afternoon. One such storm produced a severe thunderstorm wind gust of 62 mph at the Clayton Airport.||Key Word: Severe Thunderstorm Wind Gust.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Clayton Airport measured a 62 mph thunderstorm wind gust.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A trough passing over the region, a nearby dryline, and upslope surface flow allowed for the development of scattered thunderstorms across Southeast New Mexico and West Texas. Large hail was reported in Brewster County in/near the higher elevation. Some strong winds were also measured that evening. High non-thunderstorm winds were also reported in the Guadalupe Mountains overnight as the trough/Pacific front moved through.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The KCNM Carlsbad ASOS station recorded a 59 mph wind gust.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Another unseasonably deep upper trough moved into the western U.S. March 17th strengthening into a closed upper low over Colorado on March 18th. Strong south to southwest winds ahead of the trough resulted in blowing dust across southern Chaves County resulting in the closure of the Roswell Relief route on the southwest side of town during the afternoon of March 17th. High wind gusts of 60 to 80 mph began across the high terrain during the morning hours of March 18th moving down to lower elevations late morning as the Pacific front moved through the state. The subsidence behind the front helped to mix down strong mid and upper-level winds at the base of the newly developed low down to the surface. A strong pressure gradient was also present across the state south of a 990 mb surface low over eastern Colorado. These high wind gusts resulted in downed trees at Corrales in the Albuquerque Metro and in southwest Chaves County and blew a roof off a workshop near San Mateo in the west central mountains. The high winds also resulted in widespread blowing dust and near zero visibility across the Rio Grande Valley and eastern NM. The near zero visibility from the blowing dust resulted in multi-vehicle crashes on I-25 between Albuquerque and Santa Fe, I-40 in Torrance and Guadalupe County, and on U.S. Highway 285 south of Roswell. U.S. Highway 54 in the Carrizozo area was shut down for several hours due to the near zero visibility from the blowing dust. High winds from a shower induced microburst resulted in some power outages in east central Albuquerque during the mid evening. Winds and blowing dust subsided during the late evening hours across central and southern New Mexico, but continued across far northeast New Mexico behind a backdoor front until the late morning hours of March 19th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Taos Ski Valley at Kachina Peak measured a peak wind gust of 73 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level low crossing the western CONUS brought a strong jetstream and Pacific cold front across New Mexico March 3rd. Strong southwesterly winds mixed down to the surface through much of the southeastern half of the state, producing gusts of 45 to 75 mph. Blowing dust was lofted up producing low to near zero visibility in parts of Chaves and Lincoln Counties. Two multi-vehicles crashes were reported along U.S. Highway 285, one near Lake Arthur and the other involving two semi-trucks just south of Roswell. Thankfully, no loss of life was reported. Strong northerly to northwesterly winds wrapped around the backside of the upper low as it exited east of NM during the night of March 3rd through the 4th. Winter weather in the form of blowing snow impacted areas from Raton Pass to Union County before high winds filled in across eastern NM. Strong west to northwest winds gusting 60 to 80 mph were observed over areas mainly along and east of the central mountain chain. High wind damage and blowing dust produced more impacts to property and closed area highways, including area highways near and south of Roswell, NM.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A public weather station at Hartley Ranch measured a peak gust of 64 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: April saw below normal precipitation across much of the state, resulting in a significant expansion of Extreme Drought conditions, mainly along and west of the Rio Grande Valley. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Higher moisture moved into eastern New Mexico early on May 25th behind a backdoor cold front. This added moisture combined with lift ahead of a weakening upper low over Utah resulting in the development of scattered showers and storms along and east of the central mountain chain. Several of these storms became strong to severe with one producing quarter size hail near Tierra Monte in the east slopes of the Sangre de Cristo Mountains and another producing a tornado near Floyd in western Roosevelt County and quarter to ping pong ball size hail between Cannon Air Force Base and downtown Clovis. Storms across eastern New Mexico mostly tapered off around sunset while the convectively enhanced backdoor front pushed west through the gaps of the central mountain chain. ||Key Words: Severe Thunderstorm Winds, Large Hail, Tornado, Funnel Cloud.||Another round of showers and storms developed and moved across north central and eastern New Mexico during the afternoon and evening hours of May 26th due to higher surface moisture in place combining with added lift and instability from the upper level disturbance moving overhead. Penny to golf ball size hail was observed at several locations, a brief tornado was observed near Montezuma northeast of Las Vegas, and damaging straight line winds resulted in a flipped and overturned an 18-wheeler trailer near Forrest in southern Quay County. Storms exited east into Texas around sunset.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Penny size diameter hail was observed along State Highway 58 just east of Cimarron.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Denver WIT Mesonet site on the White Sands Missile Range recorded a peak gust of 60 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper-level trough dove south and closed off into a low over Arizona during the day of April 4th. Lift ahead of the low along with a backdoor front diving south and west across eastern New Mexico and into the Rio Grande Valley resulted in the development of snow across eastern, northern and west central New Mexico late April 4th into April 5th. Snow peaked in intensity, especially along the east slopes of the central mountain chain during the morning hours of April 5th due to upslope flow behind the backdoor front along with the upper low approaching the bootheel in southwest New Mexico. Snow waned slowly from north to south across central and eastern New Mexico during afternoon hours as the low moved across the southern portion of the state into West Texas. Overall, snowfall amounts of 10 to 18 inches were observed across the east slopes of the Sangre de Cristo Mountains and Sandia and Manzano Mountains, around 5 to 10 inches across the northern mountains, and 4 to 8 inches across the west central highlands and northeast and east central highlands and plains. Snow and blowing snow resulted in the closure of U.S. Highway 64/87 in far northeast NM for several hours as well as sections of I-40 from Tijeras Canyon to Santa Rosa due to several vehicle accidents.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The SNOTEL site at Wesner Springs observed an estimated 17 inches of snowfall.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Similar to May 4th, a large upper-level low pressure over the western United States and a leading shortwave trough produced scattered severe thunderstorms over the higher terrain of west Texas and southeast New Mexico, as well as in the Permian Basin. Thunderstorms continued to develop into the night and moved east of our area after midnight. Meanwhile, a strong Pacific front resulted in strong non-thunderstorm winds in the Davis and Guadalupe Mountains that night.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: LANDSPOUT. Location: 32.370,-104.170/32.380,-104.165. A short-lived landspout, lasting less than 2 minutes, was observed by the public about 5 miles southeast of Carlsbad moving north-northeastward. The landspout was approximately 25 yards in width. The track is approximate. No damage was observed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A trailing upper level disturbance moved through northern and central New Mexico on May 19th. Daytime heating resulted in the development of isolated to scattered virga showers and thunderstorms across central NM mid to late morning. This virga and shower activity resulted in high wind gusts across the central highlands, western Guadalupe County and Fort Sumner during the early to mid afternoon hours. Additionally, strong wind gusts from showers in the Albuquerque Metro resulted in down trees and tree branches in Corrales. Finally, a high wind gust of 60 mph was observed at Tucumcari Municipal Airport. Shower and storm activity exited east into Texas during the evening hours.||Key Words: Virga, High Wind Gusts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Clines Corners ASOS measured a 61 mph wind gust as showers moved through the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Abundant moisture pushed east to west across NM behind a strong cold front ahead of a strong upper level low approaching from AZ. The abundant moisture and favorable weather setup allowed for multiple days of strong to severe thunderstorms to develop across northern and central NM from May 4th through the 6th. Alongside all of this was unseasonably cold weather at higher elevations of the northern mountains. The Sangre de Cristo Mountains received 1 to 3 feet of snow at higher elevations above 9,500 feet. These high snow amounts ended up melting fairly quickly or succumbed to rain on snow events producing flooding impacts over portions of the east slopes of the Sangre de Cristo Mountains that lasted into May 8th.||Key Words: Heavy Snow, Severe Thunderstorms, River Flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Taos Powderhorn SNOTEL measured an estimated total of 24 inches of snow. Snow totals elsewhere in the zone ranged between 9 to 22 inches. The town of Red River lost power due to the heavy snow all day on May 6th.

A late season winter storm event from May 4th through the 6th dumped two to three feet of snow across the Sangre de Cristo Mountains. This event was followed by a rain on snow event from May 5th through the 8th in which flooding was experienced across a large section of the east slopes of the Sangre de Cristo Mountains from areas around Mora, to Las Vegas, to Mineral Hill. A fast warmup in temperatures continued beyond May 8th to May 11th exacerbating the snowmelt in the area, allowing for flood impacts to persist in areas around Mora to Rociada.||Key Word: Snowmelt Flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter reported that flooding from the rain on snow event that had ended on earlier in the week was continuing due to the fast warm-up in temperatures exacerbating the snowmelt. Flood impacts included streams and creeks overtopping area highways near Pendaries between Mora and Rociada and along the Sapello River.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A public weather station near Estancia measured a 59 mph wind gust as showers moved through the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Higher moisture moved into eastern New Mexico early on May 25th behind a backdoor cold front. This added moisture combined with lift ahead of a weakening upper low over Utah resulting in the development of scattered showers and storms along and east of the central mountain chain. Several of these storms became strong to severe with one producing quarter size hail near Tierra Monte in the east slopes of the Sangre de Cristo Mountains and another producing a tornado near Floyd in western Roosevelt County and quarter to ping pong ball size hail between Cannon Air Force Base and downtown Clovis. Storms across eastern New Mexico mostly tapered off around sunset while the convectively enhanced backdoor front pushed west through the gaps of the central mountain chain. ||Key Words: Severe Thunderstorm Winds, Large Hail, Tornado, Funnel Cloud.||Another round of showers and storms developed and moved across north central and eastern New Mexico during the afternoon and evening hours of May 26th due to higher surface moisture in place combining with added lift and instability from the upper level disturbance moving overhead. Penny to golf ball size hail was observed at several locations, a brief tornado was observed near Montezuma northeast of Las Vegas, and damaging straight line winds resulted in a flipped and overturned an 18-wheeler trailer near Forrest in southern Quay County. Storms exited east into Texas around sunset.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Ping pong diameter size hail was observed 5 miles southeast of Cannon Air Force Base.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper low moved across the Great Basin and Northern and Central Rockies on May 13th into May 14th with strong winds at the base of the low over New Mexico. Deep daytime mixing allowed strong winds at the base of the low to mix down to the surface mainly during the afternoon through mid-evening hours resulting in gusty winds across western New Mexico and areas along and east of the central mountain chain. A brief high wind gust of 62 mph was observed at the Las Vegas Municipal Airport and a brief 60 mph was observed at the Sevilleta RAWS during the afternoon hours on May 13th. High winds were observed across the south central mountains from the mid-morning to early afternoon hours on May 14th. The gusty winds resulted in blowing dust across southern Chaves County on May 14th during the afternoon and early evening hours resulting in the closure of State Highway 2 at Hagerman. West winds tapered off across the region around sunset.||Key Words: High Winds, Dust Storm.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Las Vegas Municipal Airport measured a brief high wind gust of 62 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level low crossing the western CONUS brought a strong jetstream and Pacific cold front across New Mexico March 3rd. Strong southwesterly winds mixed down to the surface through much of the southeastern half of the state, producing gusts of 45 to 75 mph. Blowing dust was lofted up producing low to near zero visibility in parts of Chaves and Lincoln Counties. Two multi-vehicles crashes were reported along U.S. Highway 285, one near Lake Arthur and the other involving two semi-trucks just south of Roswell. Thankfully, no loss of life was reported. Strong northerly to northwesterly winds wrapped around the backside of the upper low as it exited east of NM during the night of March 3rd through the 4th. Winter weather in the form of blowing snow impacted areas from Raton Pass to Union County before high winds filled in across eastern NM. Strong west to northwest winds gusting 60 to 80 mph were observed over areas mainly along and east of the central mountain chain. High wind damage and blowing dust produced more impacts to property and closed area highways, including area highways near and south of Roswell, NM.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The East Fort Sumner mesonet station measured a peak wind gust of 67 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A disturbance in the quasi-zonal/northwesterly flow aloft, a dryline, and a cold front moving across the northern Permian Basin allowed for the development of scattered thunderstorms across Southeast New Mexico and West Texas. Thunderstorm coverage continued to increase into the afternoon and evening, and a number of storms became severe. The strongest storm of the day was in Dawson and Howard counties. This storm latched onto the stationary boundary and produced two brief EF-1 tornadoes in Dawson County as well as damaging straight line winds and large hail. Other thunderstorms also produced large hail and damaging winds, and a landspout was reported near Loving, New Mexico.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: LANDSPOUT. Location: 32.30,-104.11/32.30,-104.10. A storm spotter reported a landspout west of Loving, New Mexico. The amount of time it lasted is estimated, as are the wind gusts associated with it. No damage was observed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper trough over the Great Basin deepened into an upper low and dug south over Arizona during the day of March 7th. Gusty west winds across Roswell resulted in more blowing dust across the Roswell Relief Route. Lift from a Pacific front ahead of the eastward moving upper low combined with a backdoor front draped across northern New Mexico resulted in the development of snow across western, northern, and east central New Mexico late March 7th though the middle of March 8th. Generally, 3 to 6 inches of snow fell across western New Mexico, around 10 to 20 inches of snow across the northern mountains and east slopes of the Sangre de Cristo Mountains, and 4 to 6 inches across the northeast and east central plains. Snow came to an end during the day of March 8th as the upper low exited east of the state.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Chacon CoCoRaHS measured 15.2 inches of snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper-level trough dove south and closed off into a low over Arizona during the day of April 4th. Lift ahead of the low along with a backdoor front diving south and west across eastern New Mexico and into the Rio Grande Valley resulted in the development of snow across eastern, northern and west central New Mexico late April 4th into April 5th. Snow peaked in intensity, especially along the east slopes of the central mountain chain during the morning hours of April 5th due to upslope flow behind the backdoor front along with the upper low approaching the bootheel in southwest New Mexico. Snow waned slowly from north to south across central and eastern New Mexico during afternoon hours as the low moved across the southern portion of the state into West Texas. Overall, snowfall amounts of 10 to 18 inches were observed across the east slopes of the Sangre de Cristo Mountains and Sandia and Manzano Mountains, around 5 to 10 inches across the northern mountains, and 4 to 8 inches across the west central highlands and northeast and east central highlands and plains. Snow and blowing snow resulted in the closure of U.S. Highway 64/87 in far northeast NM for several hours as well as sections of I-40 from Tijeras Canyon to Santa Rosa due to several vehicle accidents.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow and blowing snow resulted in the closure of U.S. 64/87 in the zone.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Similar to May 4th, a large upper-level low pressure over the western United States and a leading shortwave trough produced scattered severe thunderstorms over the higher terrain of west Texas and southeast New Mexico, as well as in the Permian Basin. Thunderstorms continued to develop into the night and moved east of our area after midnight. Meanwhile, a strong Pacific front resulted in strong non-thunderstorm winds in the Davis and Guadalupe Mountains that night.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A storm chaser reported dime to quarter-size hail.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deep upper trough developed over the Great Basin during the day on March 6th with strong mid and upper-level winds ahead of this low and over New Mexico. The upper low also induced the development of a strong 989 mb surface lee low over southeast Colorado by the afternoon. The surface gradient south of this low along with deep daytime mixing resulted in high wind gusts of 60 to 75 mph across central and eastern New Mexico from midday through the evening hours. These strong southwest to west winds combined with the dry soil conditions resulted in blowing dust resulting in near zero visibility in Albuquerque and Roswell. High winds tapered off around sunset for most areas and around midnight across the northeast highlands.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A photo shared on social media showed a plane landing in a near zero visibility brownout conditions at the Roswell Airport.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An unseasonably and record deep upper trough for mid-March moved through the intermountain West late on March 13th closing off into a deep upper low on March 14th. Strong south to southwest winds ahead of the trough resulted in blowing dust across southern Chaves County resulting in the closure of NM Highway 2 at Hagerman. The Pacific front moved through the state during the overnight hours of March 13th into March 14th. Subsidence behind the Pacific front combined with strong mid and upper-level winds at the base of this trough, a strong pressure gradient south and west of the newly formed upper low and 975 mb surface low across the central Great Plains resulted in widespread high wind gusts of 60 to 80 mph across central New Mexico from the early morning to around midday and across eastern New Mexico during the morning through mid afternoon hours. These wind gusts behind the Pacific front resulted in wind damage across several zones in eastern New Mexico along with widespread blowing dust across the Lower Rio Grande Valley and eastern New Mexico. Strong and dangerous westerly crosswinds even closed down I-25 from Las Vegas to Raton during the late morning to mid afternoon hours. Winds and blowing dust subsided during the late afternoon and early evening hours as the strong storm system and associated higher mid and upper-level winds, exited east of the state.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Little Burro White Sands Missile Range station measured a peak wind gust of 68 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Another unseasonably deep upper trough moved into the western U.S. March 17th strengthening into a closed upper low over Colorado on March 18th. Strong south to southwest winds ahead of the trough resulted in blowing dust across southern Chaves County resulting in the closure of the Roswell Relief route on the southwest side of town during the afternoon of March 17th. High wind gusts of 60 to 80 mph began across the high terrain during the morning hours of March 18th moving down to lower elevations late morning as the Pacific front moved through the state. The subsidence behind the front helped to mix down strong mid and upper-level winds at the base of the newly developed low down to the surface. A strong pressure gradient was also present across the state south of a 990 mb surface low over eastern Colorado. These high wind gusts resulted in downed trees at Corrales in the Albuquerque Metro and in southwest Chaves County and blew a roof off a workshop near San Mateo in the west central mountains. The high winds also resulted in widespread blowing dust and near zero visibility across the Rio Grande Valley and eastern NM. The near zero visibility from the blowing dust resulted in multi-vehicle crashes on I-25 between Albuquerque and Santa Fe, I-40 in Torrance and Guadalupe County, and on U.S. Highway 285 south of Roswell. U.S. Highway 54 in the Carrizozo area was shut down for several hours due to the near zero visibility from the blowing dust. High winds from a shower induced microburst resulted in some power outages in east central Albuquerque during the mid evening. Winds and blowing dust subsided during the late evening hours across central and southern New Mexico, but continued across far northeast New Mexico behind a backdoor front until the late morning hours of March 19th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Tower RAWS measured a peak wind gust of 62 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A trailing upper level disturbance moved through northern and central New Mexico on May 19th. Daytime heating resulted in the development of isolated to scattered virga showers and thunderstorms across central NM mid to late morning. This virga and shower activity resulted in high wind gusts across the central highlands, western Guadalupe County and Fort Sumner during the early to mid afternoon hours. Additionally, strong wind gusts from showers in the Albuquerque Metro resulted in down trees and tree branches in Corrales. Finally, a high wind gust of 60 mph was observed at Tucumcari Municipal Airport. Shower and storm activity exited east into Texas during the evening hours.||Key Words: Virga, High Wind Gusts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The mesonet station a few miles southeast of Vaughn measured a peak wind gust surpassing the limits of their instrumentation of 67 mph with another wind gust of 61 mph shortly thereafter as showers moved through the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Similar to May 4th, a large upper-level low pressure over the western United States and a leading shortwave trough produced scattered severe thunderstorms over the higher terrain of west Texas and southeast New Mexico, as well as in the Permian Basin. Thunderstorms continued to develop into the night and moved east of our area after midnight. Meanwhile, a strong Pacific front resulted in strong non-thunderstorm winds in the Davis and Guadalupe Mountains that night.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A report of penny-sized hail was received from mPING. The location is approximate, based on radar data.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Abundant moisture pushed east to west across NM behind a strong cold front ahead of a strong upper level low approaching from AZ. The abundant moisture and favorable weather setup allowed for multiple days of strong to severe thunderstorms to develop across northern and central NM from May 4th through the 6th. Alongside all of this was unseasonably cold weather at higher elevations of the northern mountains. The Sangre de Cristo Mountains received 1 to 3 feet of snow at higher elevations above 9,500 feet. These high snow amounts ended up melting fairly quickly or succumbed to rain on snow events producing flooding impacts over portions of the east slopes of the Sangre de Cristo Mountains that lasted into May 8th.||Key Words: Heavy Snow, Severe Thunderstorms, River Flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Penny to ping-bong ball size hail from a first round of storms.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Iron Fire in the Gila National Forest in a very remote area of southern Catron County was discovered near noon on May 4th. It quickly expanded under critical fire weather conditions that day with a final mapped acreage of 482 acres. Cooler and wetter weather allowed the fire activity to wane with no further growth occurring after May 6th. No damage has been reported, although evacuations of several nearby campgrounds was ordered.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A late season winter storm event from May 4th through the 6th dumped two to three feet of snow across the Sangre de Cristo Mountains. This event was followed by a rain on snow event from May 5th through the 8th in which flooding was experienced across a large section of the east slopes of the Sangre de Cristo Mountains from areas around Mora, to Las Vegas, to Mineral Hill. A fast warmup in temperatures continued beyond May 8th to May 11th exacerbating the snowmelt in the area, allowing for flood impacts to persist in areas around Mora to Rociada.||Key Word: Snowmelt Flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter reported that flooding from the rain on snow event that had ended on earlier in the week was continuing due to the fast warm-up in temperatures exacerbating the snowmelt. Flood impacts included streams and creeks overtopping area highways.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level low crossing the western CONUS brought a strong jetstream and Pacific cold front across New Mexico March 3rd. Strong southwesterly winds mixed down to the surface through much of the southeastern half of the state, producing gusts of 45 to 75 mph. Blowing dust was lofted up producing low to near zero visibility in parts of Chaves and Lincoln Counties. Two multi-vehicles crashes were reported along U.S. Highway 285, one near Lake Arthur and the other involving two semi-trucks just south of Roswell. Thankfully, no loss of life was reported. Strong northerly to northwesterly winds wrapped around the backside of the upper low as it exited east of NM during the night of March 3rd through the 4th. Winter weather in the form of blowing snow impacted areas from Raton Pass to Union County before high winds filled in across eastern NM. Strong west to northwest winds gusting 60 to 80 mph were observed over areas mainly along and east of the central mountain chain. High wind damage and blowing dust produced more impacts to property and closed area highways, including area highways near and south of Roswell, NM.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Nogal mesonet station measured a peak wind gust of 66 mph. Peak gusts of 60 to 62 were measured by Carrizozo mesonet and Sierra Blanca Airport AWOS stations.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deep upper trough developed over the Great Basin during the day on March 6th with strong mid and upper-level winds ahead of this low and over New Mexico. The upper low also induced the development of a strong 989 mb surface lee low over southeast Colorado by the afternoon. The surface gradient south of this low along with deep daytime mixing resulted in high wind gusts of 60 to 75 mph across central and eastern New Mexico from midday through the evening hours. These strong southwest to west winds combined with the dry soil conditions resulted in blowing dust resulting in near zero visibility in Albuquerque and Roswell. High winds tapered off around sunset for most areas and around midnight across the northeast highlands.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Estancia mesonet station measured a peak wind gust of 58 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An unseasonably and record deep upper trough for mid-March moved through the intermountain West late on March 13th closing off into a deep upper low on March 14th. Strong south to southwest winds ahead of the trough resulted in blowing dust across southern Chaves County resulting in the closure of NM Highway 2 at Hagerman. The Pacific front moved through the state during the overnight hours of March 13th into March 14th. Subsidence behind the Pacific front combined with strong mid and upper-level winds at the base of this trough, a strong pressure gradient south and west of the newly formed upper low and 975 mb surface low across the central Great Plains resulted in widespread high wind gusts of 60 to 80 mph across central New Mexico from the early morning to around midday and across eastern New Mexico during the morning through mid afternoon hours. These wind gusts behind the Pacific front resulted in wind damage across several zones in eastern New Mexico along with widespread blowing dust across the Lower Rio Grande Valley and eastern New Mexico. Strong and dangerous westerly crosswinds even closed down I-25 from Las Vegas to Raton during the late morning to mid afternoon hours. Winds and blowing dust subsided during the late afternoon and early evening hours as the strong storm system and associated higher mid and upper-level winds, exited east of the state.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Clines Corners ASOS station measured a peak wind gust of 67 mph, and a high sustained wind speed of 41 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Another unseasonably deep upper trough moved into the western U.S. March 17th strengthening into a closed upper low over Colorado on March 18th. Strong south to southwest winds ahead of the trough resulted in blowing dust across southern Chaves County resulting in the closure of the Roswell Relief route on the southwest side of town during the afternoon of March 17th. High wind gusts of 60 to 80 mph began across the high terrain during the morning hours of March 18th moving down to lower elevations late morning as the Pacific front moved through the state. The subsidence behind the front helped to mix down strong mid and upper-level winds at the base of the newly developed low down to the surface. A strong pressure gradient was also present across the state south of a 990 mb surface low over eastern Colorado. These high wind gusts resulted in downed trees at Corrales in the Albuquerque Metro and in southwest Chaves County and blew a roof off a workshop near San Mateo in the west central mountains. The high winds also resulted in widespread blowing dust and near zero visibility across the Rio Grande Valley and eastern NM. The near zero visibility from the blowing dust resulted in multi-vehicle crashes on I-25 between Albuquerque and Santa Fe, I-40 in Torrance and Guadalupe County, and on U.S. Highway 285 south of Roswell. U.S. Highway 54 in the Carrizozo area was shut down for several hours due to the near zero visibility from the blowing dust. High winds from a shower induced microburst resulted in some power outages in east central Albuquerque during the mid evening. Winds and blowing dust subsided during the late evening hours across central and southern New Mexico, but continued across far northeast New Mexico behind a backdoor front until the late morning hours of March 19th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Magdalena Ridge Observatory measured a peak wind gust of 82 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper trough over the Great Basin deepened into an upper low and dug south over Arizona during the day of March 7th. Gusty west winds across Roswell resulted in more blowing dust across the Roswell Relief Route. Lift from a Pacific front ahead of the eastward moving upper low combined with a backdoor front draped across northern New Mexico resulted in the development of snow across western, northern, and east central New Mexico late March 7th though the middle of March 8th. Generally, 3 to 6 inches of snow fell across western New Mexico, around 10 to 20 inches of snow across the northern mountains and east slopes of the Sangre de Cristo Mountains, and 4 to 6 inches across the northeast and east central plains. Snow came to an end during the day of March 8th as the upper low exited east of the state.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Miami CoCoRaHS measured 12.3 inches of snow. Amounts elsewhere in the zone ranged from 2 to 8 inches.

The Gallinas Creek in particular responded with high rises that flooded several low water crossings within Las Vegas, NM. Videos shared on Facebook showed flooding in Sapello, NM with other reports of flooding near Mineral Hill west of Las Vegas.

CZ_NAME: SAN AGUSTIN PLAINS AND ADJACENT LOWLANDS
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Overall drought conditions continued to worsen and expand across northern and central NM due to above normal temperatures and well below normal precipitation. Several storms brought high winds, high fire danger and widespread blowing dust due to the antecedent dry conditions in place. The high winds from these storms also helped play a role in worsening drought conditions across the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Extreme drought conditions were introduced to much of the zone with severe drought conditions continuing across the rest of the zone.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A disturbance in the quasi-zonal/northwesterly flow aloft, a dryline, and a cold front moving across the northern Permian Basin allowed for the development of scattered thunderstorms across Southeast New Mexico and West Texas. Thunderstorm coverage continued to increase into the afternoon and evening, and a number of storms became severe. The strongest storm of the day was in Dawson and Howard counties. This storm latched onto the stationary boundary and produced two brief EF-1 tornadoes in Dawson County as well as damaging straight line winds and large hail. Other thunderstorms also produced large hail and damaging winds, and a landspout was reported near Loving, New Mexico.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Mesonet station 51 2SW Tatum recorded a 58 mph wind gust.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper low moved across the Great Basin and Northern and Central Rockies on May 13th into May 14th with strong winds at the base of the low over New Mexico. Deep daytime mixing allowed strong winds at the base of the low to mix down to the surface mainly during the afternoon through mid-evening hours resulting in gusty winds across western New Mexico and areas along and east of the central mountain chain. A brief high wind gust of 62 mph was observed at the Las Vegas Municipal Airport and a brief 60 mph was observed at the Sevilleta RAWS during the afternoon hours on May 13th. High winds were observed across the south central mountains from the mid-morning to early afternoon hours on May 14th. The gusty winds resulted in blowing dust across southern Chaves County on May 14th during the afternoon and early evening hours resulting in the closure of State Highway 2 at Hagerman. West winds tapered off across the region around sunset.||Key Words: High Winds, Dust Storm.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Sevilleta RAWS measured a brief wind gust of 60 mph.

CZ_NAME: MIDDLE RIO GRANDE VALLEY/ALBUQUERQUE METRO AREA
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An unseasonably and record deep upper trough for mid-March moved through the intermountain West late on March 13th closing off into a deep upper low on March 14th. Strong south to southwest winds ahead of the trough resulted in blowing dust across southern Chaves County resulting in the closure of NM Highway 2 at Hagerman. The Pacific front moved through the state during the overnight hours of March 13th into March 14th. Subsidence behind the Pacific front combined with strong mid and upper-level winds at the base of this trough, a strong pressure gradient south and west of the newly formed upper low and 975 mb surface low across the central Great Plains resulted in widespread high wind gusts of 60 to 80 mph across central New Mexico from the early morning to around midday and across eastern New Mexico during the morning through mid afternoon hours. These wind gusts behind the Pacific front resulted in wind damage across several zones in eastern New Mexico along with widespread blowing dust across the Lower Rio Grande Valley and eastern New Mexico. Strong and dangerous westerly crosswinds even closed down I-25 from Las Vegas to Raton during the late morning to mid afternoon hours. Winds and blowing dust subsided during the late afternoon and early evening hours as the strong storm system and associated higher mid and upper-level winds, exited east of the state.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Albuquerque Airport ASOS measured a peak wind gust of 62 mph. A truck with camper trailer was also flipped over along I-25 near Los Lunas, reported by the Isleta Pueblo Fire Department.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Overall drought conditions continued to worsen and expand across northern and central NM due to above normal temperatures and well below normal precipitation. Several storms brought high winds, high fire danger and widespread blowing dust due to the antecedent dry conditions in place. The high winds from these storms also helped play a role in worsening drought conditions across the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Severe drought conditions were introduced to southern two thirds section of the zone.

CZ_NAME: EAST SLOPES SANGRE DE CRISTO MOUNTAINS
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Another unseasonably deep upper trough moved into the western U.S. March 17th strengthening into a closed upper low over Colorado on March 18th. Strong south to southwest winds ahead of the trough resulted in blowing dust across southern Chaves County resulting in the closure of the Roswell Relief route on the southwest side of town during the afternoon of March 17th. High wind gusts of 60 to 80 mph began across the high terrain during the morning hours of March 18th moving down to lower elevations late morning as the Pacific front moved through the state. The subsidence behind the front helped to mix down strong mid and upper-level winds at the base of the newly developed low down to the surface. A strong pressure gradient was also present across the state south of a 990 mb surface low over eastern Colorado. These high wind gusts resulted in downed trees at Corrales in the Albuquerque Metro and in southwest Chaves County and blew a roof off a workshop near San Mateo in the west central mountains. The high winds also resulted in widespread blowing dust and near zero visibility across the Rio Grande Valley and eastern NM. The near zero visibility from the blowing dust resulted in multi-vehicle crashes on I-25 between Albuquerque and Santa Fe, I-40 in Torrance and Guadalupe County, and on U.S. Highway 285 south of Roswell. U.S. Highway 54 in the Carrizozo area was shut down for several hours due to the near zero visibility from the blowing dust. High winds from a shower induced microburst resulted in some power outages in east central Albuquerque during the mid evening. Winds and blowing dust subsided during the late evening hours across central and southern New Mexico, but continued across far northeast New Mexico behind a backdoor front until the late morning hours of March 19th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Angel Fire Airport measured a peak wind gust of 73 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level low crossing the western CONUS brought a strong jetstream and Pacific cold front across New Mexico March 3rd. Strong southwesterly winds mixed down to the surface through much of the southeastern half of the state, producing gusts of 45 to 75 mph. Blowing dust was lofted up producing low to near zero visibility in parts of Chaves and Lincoln Counties. Two multi-vehicles crashes were reported along U.S. Highway 285, one near Lake Arthur and the other involving two semi-trucks just south of Roswell. Thankfully, no loss of life was reported. Strong northerly to northwesterly winds wrapped around the backside of the upper low as it exited east of NM during the night of March 3rd through the 4th. Winter weather in the form of blowing snow impacted areas from Raton Pass to Union County before high winds filled in across eastern NM. Strong west to northwest winds gusting 60 to 80 mph were observed over areas mainly along and east of the central mountain chain. High wind damage and blowing dust produced more impacts to property and closed area highways, including area highways near and south of Roswell, NM.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The southeast Vaughn mesonet station measured a peak gust surpassing its wind speed threshold of 67 mph. Another mesonet station near Vaughn measured a 59 mph wind gust.
