EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deep upper trough developed over the Great Basin during the day on March 6th with strong mid and upper-level winds ahead of this low and over New Mexico. The upper low also induced the development of a strong 989 mb surface lee low over southeast Colorado by the afternoon. The surface gradient south of this low along with deep daytime mixing resulted in high wind gusts of 60 to 75 mph across central and eastern New Mexico from midday through the evening hours. These strong southwest to west winds combined with the dry soil conditions resulted in blowing dust resulting in near zero visibility in Albuquerque and Roswell. High winds tapered off around sunset for most areas and around midnight across the northeast highlands.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Oscura Range Camp and Phillips Hill White Sands Missile Range stations measured a peak wind gusts of 65 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: April saw below normal precipitation across much of the state, resulting in a significant expansion of Extreme Drought conditions, mainly along and west of the Rio Grande Valley. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A disturbance in the quasi-zonal/northwesterly flow aloft, a dryline, and a cold front moving across the northern Permian Basin allowed for the development of scattered thunderstorms across Southeast New Mexico and West Texas. Thunderstorm coverage continued to increase into the afternoon and evening, and a number of storms became severe. The strongest storm of the day was in Dawson and Howard counties. This storm latched onto the stationary boundary and produced two brief EF-1 tornadoes in Dawson County as well as damaging straight line winds and large hail. Other thunderstorms also produced large hail and damaging winds, and a landspout was reported near Loving, New Mexico.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Roadway and street flooding in/around Tatum was reported by emergency management. The timing is approximated by radar. No damage was reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Daytime heating combined with higher moisture east of a dryline and upper level northwest flow across the northern and central Rockies and Great Plains resulted in the development of severe storms with large hail across Colfax, Union and far northeast Harding Counties during the late afternoon and evening hours before exiting into West Texas after midnight. A more potent shortwave at the base of an upper low over the central U.S. and Canadian border moved across the central and southern Great Plains on June 8th resulting in the development of strong to severe storms across northeast and east central NM during the mid afternoon and evening hours until exiting into Texas at around sunset. Finally, a 61 mph wind gust was observed at Raton Crews Airport during the evening hours of June 11th due to a nearby thunderstorm.||These storms pushed a backdoor front and higher moisture south and west across eastern and central NM resulting in the development of another round of strong to severe storms across these parts of the state during the afternoon hours of June 9th. Storms organized into a convective line across eastern New Mexico during the evening hours producing damaging wind gusts of 60 to 70 mph across parts of the east central plains. This complex of storms exited into Texas just before midnight. Daytime heating combined with an upper level disturbance moving into southern New Mexico resulted in the development of some strong thunderstorms across the higher terrain. Thunderstorms across the Sacramento Mountains dropped heavy rainfall in a short period of time on the South Fork and Salt burn scars around Ruidoso resulting in some flash flooding and debris flows in the Upper Canyon area just west of downtown Ruidoso. These storms combined into a larger complex of storms over Chaves County before exiting into West Texas during the early evening hours.||Key Impacts: Hail, Severe Thunderstorm Wind Gust, Flash Flooding, Debris Flow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter just east of House in Quay County reported broken tree branches of about 5 inches in diameter around her house caused by damaging wind gusts from a cluster of severe thunderstorms.

One of these virga showers resulted in a brief 58 mph wind gust at Sierra Blanca Regional Airport. Dry and breezy southwest winds quickly developed across the entire region in the wake of the exiting disturbance.||Key Words; Virga, High Wind Gust.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A virga shower resulted in a brief 58 mph wind gust at Sierra Blanca Regional Airport.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deep upper level trough moved from the Great Basin into the central Rockies on May 18th. Strong mid to upper level winds at the base of the trough moved over New Mexico during this time. Deep daytime mixing along with the development of a 992 mb surface low over eastern Colorado resulted in gusty southwest to west winds across much of the state. Brief high wind gusts were observed at a few sites across the central and northeast highlands during the early to mid afternoon hours. Southwest to west winds tapered off across the region at around sunset.||Key Words: High Wind Gusts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Clines Corners ASOS measured a brief wind gust of 58 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Moist upslope flow, coupled with shortwave pulses in the northwesterly flow aloft, allowed for the development of scattered thunderstorms in Southeast New Mexico. One of these storms produced a 69 mph wind gust near Carlsbad Caverns before it moved into West Texas and dissipated.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The West Texas Mesonet station at Carlsbad Caverns NP measured a 69 mph wind gust.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak shortwave trough rounding an upper-level ridge and upslope flow resulted in scattered thunderstorms over the mountains. A few of these thunderstorms produced strong to severe wind gusts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A maximum wind gust of 66 MPH was recorded at the AWOS Station, KATS Artesia.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak shortwave trough rounding an upper-level ridge and upslope flow resulted in scattered thunderstorms over the mountains. A few of these thunderstorms produced strong to severe wind gusts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A maximum wind gust of 69 MPH was recorded at the ASOS Station, KCNM Carlsbad.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Isolated showers and thunderstorms developed over the mountains with the aid of upslope flow. A localized microburst occurred with one of these thunderstorms near Carlsbad, resulting in severe winds and reports of damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Damaging winds from a microburst caused significant damage to The Living Desert Zoo and Garden State Park in western Carlsbad. Several large trees were broken, fencing was damaged, and a concrete retaining wall was toppled. Time is estimated, and winds are estimated to have been up to 80 MPH. The property damage cost is also an estimation.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Isolated showers and thunderstorms developed over the mountains with the aid of upslope flow. A localized microburst occurred with one of these thunderstorms near Carlsbad, resulting in severe winds and reports of damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A maximum wind gust of 76 MPH was recorded at the ASOS station, KCNM Carlsbad.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A continued monsoonal weather pattern resulted in scattered, slow-moving thunderstorms across the region. Localized flash flooding was reported in low-lying areas of I-10 and within normally dry draws.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flash flooding of the normally dry Fourmile Draw with 4 to 5 feet of water over North Lake Road. Duration is an estimation.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Shortwave troughing and upslope flow generated scattered thunderstorms in the higher terrain of West Texas. Meanwhile, troughing and a stationary front/outflow boundary helped generate thunderstorms across portions of Southeast New Mexico. A number of these storms became severe, producing damaging winds (including microbursts) and large hail. A landspout was reported near Loving, and flooding was also reported (especially in Pecos County).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Golf ball-size hail was reported by a trained spotter near the junction of NM 529 and US  62. The time of occurrence is estimated from radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Overall drought conditions continued to worsen and expand across northern and central NM due to above normal temperatures and well below normal precipitation. Several storms brought high winds, high fire danger and widespread blowing dust due to the antecedent dry conditions in place. The high winds from these storms also helped play a role in worsening drought conditions across the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Extreme drought conditions introduced to the far southern portions of the zone.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: April saw below normal precipitation across much of the state, resulting in a significant expansion of Extreme Drought conditions, mainly along and west of the Rio Grande Valley. The below normal precipitation combined with several strong wind events allowed for the worsening drought conditions.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Extreme drought was introduced to the southwestern tier of the zone.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Higher moisture moved into eastern New Mexico early on May 25th behind a backdoor cold front. This added moisture combined with lift ahead of a weakening upper low over Utah resulting in the development of scattered showers and storms along and east of the central mountain chain. Several of these storms became strong to severe with one producing quarter size hail near Tierra Monte in the east slopes of the Sangre de Cristo Mountains and another producing a tornado near Floyd in western Roosevelt County and quarter to ping pong ball size hail between Cannon Air Force Base and downtown Clovis. Storms across eastern New Mexico mostly tapered off around sunset while the convectively enhanced backdoor front pushed west through the gaps of the central mountain chain. ||Key Words: Severe Thunderstorm Winds, Large Hail, Tornado, Funnel Cloud.||Another round of showers and storms developed and moved across north central and eastern New Mexico during the afternoon and evening hours of May 26th due to higher surface moisture in place combining with added lift and instability from the upper level disturbance moving overhead. Penny to golf ball size hail was observed at several locations, a brief tornado was observed near Montezuma northeast of Las Vegas, and damaging straight line winds resulted in a flipped and overturned an 18-wheeler trailer near Forrest in southern Quay County. Storms exited east into Texas around sunset.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An observer on State Highway 209 at mile marker 76 looking east southeast observed a funnel cloud on the mesa south of Tucumcari.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Daytime heating combined with higher moisture east of a dryline and upper level northwest flow across the northern and central Rockies and Great Plains resulted in the development of severe storms with large hail across Colfax, Union and far northeast Harding Counties during the late afternoon and evening hours before exiting into West Texas after midnight. A more potent shortwave at the base of an upper low over the central U.S. and Canadian border moved across the central and southern Great Plains on June 8th resulting in the development of strong to severe storms across northeast and east central NM during the mid afternoon and evening hours until exiting into Texas at around sunset. Finally, a 61 mph wind gust was observed at Raton Crews Airport during the evening hours of June 11th due to a nearby thunderstorm.||These storms pushed a backdoor front and higher moisture south and west across eastern and central NM resulting in the development of another round of strong to severe storms across these parts of the state during the afternoon hours of June 9th. Storms organized into a convective line across eastern New Mexico during the evening hours producing damaging wind gusts of 60 to 70 mph across parts of the east central plains. This complex of storms exited into Texas just before midnight. Daytime heating combined with an upper level disturbance moving into southern New Mexico resulted in the development of some strong thunderstorms across the higher terrain. Thunderstorms across the Sacramento Mountains dropped heavy rainfall in a short period of time on the South Fork and Salt burn scars around Ruidoso resulting in some flash flooding and debris flows in the Upper Canyon area just west of downtown Ruidoso. These storms combined into a larger complex of storms over Chaves County before exiting into West Texas during the early evening hours.||Key Impacts: Hail, Severe Thunderstorm Wind Gust, Flash Flooding, Debris Flow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Penny size hail was observed in Eagle Nest from a strong thunderstorm.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Upslope easterly flow and higher surface moisture across the central mountain chain and eastern New Mexico combined with daytime heating to result in the development of showers and thunderstorms during the afternoon and evening hours. One of these thunderstorms produced pea to penny size hail in Des Moines. A backdoor front pushed through eastern New Mexico on May 29th replenishing surface moisture and providing extra lift for the development of another round of showers and thunderstorms during the afternoon and evening hours across this part of the state. One thunderstorm dropped penny size hail in rural eastern Lincoln County for a brief period of time. Shower and thunderstorm activity tapered off shortly after sunset with the loss of daytime heating.||Key Word: Hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter in rural eastern Lincoln County reported soft penny size hail.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Very dry conditions highlighted by low humidity coupled with breezy afternoon conditions produced elevated to critical fire weather conditions in the areas of southwestern and south-central NM on May 25th and 26th. Two fire starts were discovered, the Camp Fire near Fort Stanton on the 25th and the Paradise Fire on the 26th. These fires grew rapidly in their initial stages, but were quickly responded to and put out within two to three days. The Camp Fire caused damage to five structures within the Fort Stanton Historical Site, including destroying two wooden buildings.||Key Word: Wildfires.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Camp Fire was discovered near Fort Stanton, NM on May 25th and quickly grew to a few hundred acres in size on its first day. In total, it burned 877 acres through May 27th. It damaged five structures within the Fort Stanton Historical Site, including the destruction of two wooden outbuildings, and the roof on the gymnasium built in the 1940s. The cause was still unknown.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed over the central portion of the 2022 Hermit's Peak/Calf Canyon burn scar in the early afternoon of May 31st. One of these storms produced heavy rain over upper portions of the Gallinas and Sapello Rivers. An estimated 1 to 2 inches of rainfall produced flash flooding within these drainages that overtopped low water crossings that reached down the Gallinas Creek to areas between Montezuma and Las Vegas.||Key Word: Burn Scar Flash Flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The San Miguel County Fire Chief shared delayed reports of flash flooding occurring in several locations of the Hermit's Peak/Calf Canyon burn scar from a thunderstorm. Water flowing over roadways was reported southwest of Sapello at County Roads A2 and A3 near Las Dispensas, County Roads A18 and A19 near Mineral Hill, and over Cunningham Rd. and El Llano Rd. crossing Gallinas Peak between Montezuma and Las Vegas.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The month of May on the whole saw near to below normal temperatures and near to above normal precipitation across the majority of New Mexico, notably the northeastern half to two-thirds of the state. Portions of south-central and southwestern NM however saw near to slightly above normal temperatures and near to slightly below normal precipitation allowing for the introduction of exceptional drought in Catron and Socorro Counties. Because of the severe to exceptional drought conditions and high fire risk across western and central New Mexico, the state governor declared a drought emergency for the entire state om May 22nd. The Navajo Nation followed suit and declared a drought emergency on May 30th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Extreme drought persisted over the southwestern tier of the zone.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: After some areas received above normal rainfall in July, some improvement was noted in southwest New Mexico during the month of August, especially east of the Rio Grande.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The month started out with the entire zone in Extreme(D3) drought but improved to Severe(D2) drought by the end of the month.  The Cooperative Observer at New Mexico State University received 0.72 inches of rainfall which was 1.01 inches below normal.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: After some areas received above normal rainfall in July, some improvement was noted in southwest New Mexico during the month of August, especially east of the Rio Grande.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The zone started out with Exceptional(D4) drought over the west and Extreme(D3) drought over the east. By the end of the month, the west remained in Exceptional(D4) drought but as you headed east, areas improved to Severe(D2) drought.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: After some areas received above normal rainfall in July, some improvement was noted in southwest New Mexico during the month of August, especially east of the Rio Grande.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: August started out with Extreme(D3) drought over the west and Severe(D2) over the east.  By the end of the month, the west improved to Severe(D2) drought while the east improved to Moderate(D1) drought.  The White Sands National Park Cooperative Observer reported 1.24 inches of precipitation which was 0.64 inches below normal.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Upslope easterly flow and higher surface moisture across the central mountain chain and eastern New Mexico combined with daytime heating to result in the development of showers and thunderstorms during the afternoon and evening hours. One of these thunderstorms produced pea to penny size hail in Des Moines. A backdoor front pushed through eastern New Mexico on May 29th replenishing surface moisture and providing extra lift for the development of another round of showers and thunderstorms during the afternoon and evening hours across this part of the state. One thunderstorm dropped penny size hail in rural eastern Lincoln County for a brief period of time. Shower and thunderstorm activity tapered off shortly after sunset with the loss of daytime heating.||Key Word: Hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Pea to penny sized hail was observed in Des Moines. Report relayed from a broadcast station based out of Amarillo.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The month of May on the whole saw near to below normal temperatures and near to above normal precipitation across the majority of New Mexico, notably the northeastern half to two-thirds of the state. Portions of south-central and southwestern NM however saw near to slightly above normal temperatures and near to slightly below normal precipitation allowing for the introduction of exceptional drought in Catron and Socorro Counties. Because of the severe to exceptional drought conditions and high fire risk across western and central New Mexico, the state governor declared a drought emergency for the entire state om May 22nd. The Navajo Nation followed suit and declared a drought emergency on May 30th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Extreme drought persisted over the eastern third of the zone.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Higher moisture moved into eastern New Mexico early on May 25th behind a backdoor cold front. This added moisture combined with lift ahead of a weakening upper low over Utah resulting in the development of scattered showers and storms along and east of the central mountain chain. Several of these storms became strong to severe with one producing quarter size hail near Tierra Monte in the east slopes of the Sangre de Cristo Mountains and another producing a tornado near Floyd in western Roosevelt County and quarter to ping pong ball size hail between Cannon Air Force Base and downtown Clovis. Storms across eastern New Mexico mostly tapered off around sunset while the convectively enhanced backdoor front pushed west through the gaps of the central mountain chain. ||Key Words: Severe Thunderstorm Winds, Large Hail, Tornado, Funnel Cloud.||Another round of showers and storms developed and moved across north central and eastern New Mexico during the afternoon and evening hours of May 26th due to higher surface moisture in place combining with added lift and instability from the upper level disturbance moving overhead. Penny to golf ball size hail was observed at several locations, a brief tornado was observed near Montezuma northeast of Las Vegas, and damaging straight line winds resulted in a flipped and overturned an 18-wheeler trailer near Forrest in southern Quay County. Storms exited east into Texas around sunset.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Quay County emergency manager observed a funnel cloud over open farmland near Forrest, New Mexico.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The West Texas Mesonet station 5 NW Hobbs NM measured a 69 mph wind gust.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deep upper level trough moved from the Great Basin into the central Rockies on May 18th. Strong mid to upper level winds at the base of the trough moved over New Mexico during this time. Deep daytime mixing along with the development of a 992 mb surface low over eastern Colorado resulted in gusty southwest to west winds across much of the state. Brief high wind gusts were observed at a few sites across the central and northeast highlands during the early to mid afternoon hours. Southwest to west winds tapered off across the region at around sunset.||Key Words: High Wind Gusts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A public station along the Union Pacific Railroad east of Vaughn measured a brief wind gust of 60 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper high right over southern New Mexico trapped low level monsoonal moisture under it.  Overnight and afternoon thunderstorms were slow moving and heavy rain caused flash flooding near Rincon and La Union.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Dona Ana County Emergency Manager and Flood Commission reported significant water flow in the Rincon Arroyo at Interstate 25.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Monsoonal moisture remained over the region with a low level convergent area setting up near the New Mexico/Arizona border region.  A strong thunderstorm developed over the Lordsburg Playa and produced heavy rain and areas of blowing dust reducing visibilities to under a quarter mile.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A haboob reduced visibilities to an eighth of a mile along Interstate 10 between mile markers 7 and 12.  Visibilities of a quarter mile were widespread from Lordsburg to the Arizona State line.

This added moisture combined with lift ahead of a weakening upper low over Utah resulting in the development of scattered showers and storms along and east of the central mountain chain. Several of these storms became strong to severe with one producing quarter size hail near Tierra Monte in the east slopes of the Sangre de Cristo Mountains and another producing a tornado near Floyd in western Roosevelt County and quarter to ping pong ball size hail between Cannon Air Force Base and downtown Clovis. Storms across eastern New Mexico mostly tapered off around sunset while the convectively enhanced backdoor front pushed west through the gaps of the central mountain chain. ||Key Words: Severe Thunderstorm Winds, Large Hail, Tornado, Funnel Cloud.||Another round of showers and storms developed and moved across north central and eastern New Mexico during the afternoon and evening hours of May 26th due to higher surface moisture in place combining with added lift and instability from the upper level disturbance moving overhead. Penny to golf ball size hail was observed at several locations, a brief tornado was observed near Montezuma northeast of Las Vegas, and damaging straight line winds resulted in a flipped and overturned an 18-wheeler trailer near Forrest in southern Quay County. Storms exited east into Texas around sunset.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Overall rating is EF-1 with 90 mph windspeeds based on what damage indicators there were from power pole damage as no structures were impacted.  Damage survey found tree damage at the start of the tornado about 3 miles SW of Floyd before tracking northeast over open fields and crossing road NM-267 about 1.5 miles SE of Floyd. The tornado snapped 10 to 15 power poles near the bases of the poles as it continued to move northeast. Another 20 power poles were snapped on Roosevelt Road 3. This stretch of the tornado track includes its peak intensity and width with low end EF-1 damage (winds speeds near 90 mph). The tornado weakened as it tracked NE towards Roosevelt Road 2 and a dairy plant. There was a mangled farm sprinkler system to the SW of the dairy and minor damage to fences and metal roofing at the dairy plant.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper low moved across the desert southwest June 1st into June 2nd helping to pull up higher moisture from former Tropical Storm Alvin in the eastern Pacific. Widespread showers and storms developed across New Mexico during the day June 2nd as the upper low moved into the state. Storms resulted in severe wind gusts in Clovis, penny size hail and flash flooding in Albuquerque and Farmington. Flash flooding in the north diversion arroyo channel in east central Albuquerque resulted in a fatality. Storms across the state tapered off after sunset with the loss of daytime heating and the system exiting east of the state. A 2nd upper low approached the state on June 3rd resulting in the diurnal development of storms across the Four Corners area. Storms brought quarter size hail and flash flooding to the Farmington area. ||Another round of showers and storms developed across western NM on June 4th as the upper low entered the state. These storms moved east across the state bringing flash flooding and large hail to parts of the middle Rio Grande Valley and east central New Mexico during the afternoon and evening hours before exiting into the Texas Panhandle just before midnight. More rounds of severe storms developed along the dryline across far eastern New Mexico during the late afternoon and evening hours of June 5th and 6th. These storms brought large hail 1 to 2 inches in diameter to Curry and Roosevelt Counties both days and western Union County on June 6th. Additionally, several brief tornadoes were observed across east central and southeast Roosevelt County on June 5th.||Key Impacts: Hail, Flash Flood, Tornado, Severe Thunderstorm Wind Gust.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Penny to quarter size hail was reported along NM 206 south of Portales from a severe thunderstorm.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: There was continued broad troughing across the western United States with a dryline across portions of the Permian Basin south across the Davis Mountains. Splitting supercells occurred with severe weather and blowing dust from thunderstorm outflow across portions of Southeast New Mexico and the Stockton Plateau.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter shared video of a large plume of dust moving into Carlsbad in association with thunderstorm outflow. The Carlsbad Caverns ASOS station also reported significantly reduced visibility. Time is estimated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Daytime heating combined with higher moisture east of a dryline and upper level northwest flow across the northern and central Rockies and Great Plains resulted in the development of severe storms with large hail across Colfax, Union and far northeast Harding Counties during the late afternoon and evening hours before exiting into West Texas after midnight. A more potent shortwave at the base of an upper low over the central U.S. and Canadian border moved across the central and southern Great Plains on June 8th resulting in the development of strong to severe storms across northeast and east central NM during the mid afternoon and evening hours until exiting into Texas at around sunset. Finally, a 61 mph wind gust was observed at Raton Crews Airport during the evening hours of June 11th due to a nearby thunderstorm.||These storms pushed a backdoor front and higher moisture south and west across eastern and central NM resulting in the development of another round of strong to severe storms across these parts of the state during the afternoon hours of June 9th. Storms organized into a convective line across eastern New Mexico during the evening hours producing damaging wind gusts of 60 to 70 mph across parts of the east central plains. This complex of storms exited into Texas just before midnight. Daytime heating combined with an upper level disturbance moving into southern New Mexico resulted in the development of some strong thunderstorms across the higher terrain. Thunderstorms across the Sacramento Mountains dropped heavy rainfall in a short period of time on the South Fork and Salt burn scars around Ruidoso resulting in some flash flooding and debris flows in the Upper Canyon area just west of downtown Ruidoso. These storms combined into a larger complex of storms over Chaves County before exiting into West Texas during the early evening hours.||Key Impacts: Hail, Severe Thunderstorm Wind Gust, Flash Flooding, Debris Flow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several observers in Sandia Park observed hail ranging from 1/2 inch to half dollar size in Sandia Park along NM 14 from a severe thunderstorm.

Widespread showers and storms developed across New Mexico during the day June 2nd as the upper low moved into the state. Storms resulted in severe wind gusts in Clovis, penny size hail and flash flooding in Albuquerque and Farmington. Flash flooding in the north diversion arroyo channel in east central Albuquerque resulted in a fatality. Storms across the state tapered off after sunset with the loss of daytime heating and the system exiting east of the state. A 2nd upper low approached the state on June 3rd resulting in the diurnal development of storms across the Four Corners area. Storms brought quarter size hail and flash flooding to the Farmington area. ||Another round of showers and storms developed across western NM on June 4th as the upper low entered the state. These storms moved east across the state bringing flash flooding and large hail to parts of the middle Rio Grande Valley and east central New Mexico during the afternoon and evening hours before exiting into the Texas Panhandle just before midnight. More rounds of severe storms developed along the dryline across far eastern New Mexico during the late afternoon and evening hours of June 5th and 6th. These storms brought large hail 1 to 2 inches in diameter to Curry and Roosevelt Counties both days and western Union County on June 6th. Additionally, several brief tornadoes were observed across east central and southeast Roosevelt County on June 5th.||Key Impacts: Hail, Flash Flood, Tornado, Severe Thunderstorm Wind Gust.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous reports of water, debris, rocks, and mud over roadways, prompting closures.  Several portions of NM 170, La Plata Highway, and N 36, Upper Fruitland Highway, were impacted.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Daytime heating combined with higher moisture east of a dryline and upper level northwest flow across the northern and central Rockies and Great Plains resulted in the development of severe storms with large hail across Colfax, Union and far northeast Harding Counties during the late afternoon and evening hours before exiting into West Texas after midnight. EVENT_NARRATIVE: There are multiple public reports with pictures and videos of shattered windows caused by wind-driven hail approximately the size of a golf balls. The time is estimated from radar. The property damage cost is an estimation.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Tennis ball size hail was reported by a trained spotter. The time is estimated from radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A broad upper level trough was moving through the Western United States with a 100+ knot jet moving through New Mexico.  This combined with a strong surface low in southeast Colorado brought wind gusts to 96 mph to the area. Some areas across southern New Mexico also experienced dust storms with visibilities below a quarter mile causing interstates and highways to close.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A peak gust of 67 mph was recorded at the Truth or Consequences Airport.  An AWOS site 19 miles east-southeast of T or C recorded a gust to 61 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A broad upper level trough was moving through the Western United States with a 100+ knot jet moving through New Mexico.  This combined with a strong surface low in southeast Colorado brought wind gusts to 96 mph to the area. Some areas across southern New Mexico also experienced dust storms with visibilities below a quarter mile causing interstates and highways to close.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Reports around Luna county included near zero visibility along NM-11 at Sunshine as well as around the Columbus Port of Entry. Highway 9 was closed between Columbus and Santa Teresa while Highway 11 was closed between Columbus and Sunshine.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A broad upper level trough was moving through the Western United States with a 100+ knot jet moving through New Mexico.  This combined with a strong surface low in southeast Colorado brought wind gusts to 96 mph to the area. Some areas across southern New Mexico also experienced dust storms with visibilities below a quarter mile causing interstates and highways to close.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Visibility sensors along the Lordsburg Playa reported visibilities under a half mile. Interstate 10 was closed from the State Line to Lordsburg due to near zero visibilities.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong positively tilted trough was moving through the Great Basin.  Ahead of this system, gusty southwest winds develop over southern New Mexico and brought areas of blowing dust to Luna, Hidalgo and Otero counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Widespread dust swept across Luna county causing near zero visibilities, several accidents including 5 injuries and 1 fatality were reported.  Two separate wrecks occurred on Interstate 10 between mile markers 93 and 94 due to dust.  One of these wrecks caused a fatality. Another wreck was reported on NM-549 near mile marker nine which caused 4 injuries.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Daytime heating combined with higher moisture east of a dryline and upper level northwest flow across the northern and central Rockies and Great Plains resulted in the development of severe storms with large hail across Colfax, Union and far northeast Harding Counties during the late afternoon and evening hours before exiting into West Texas after midnight. A more potent shortwave at the base of an upper low over the central U.S. and Canadian border moved across the central and southern Great Plains on June 8th resulting in the development of strong to severe storms across northeast and east central NM during the mid afternoon and evening hours until exiting into Texas at around sunset. Finally, a 61 mph wind gust was observed at Raton Crews Airport during the evening hours of June 11th due to a nearby thunderstorm.||These storms pushed a backdoor front and higher moisture south and west across eastern and central NM resulting in the development of another round of strong to severe storms across these parts of the state during the afternoon hours of June 9th. Storms organized into a convective line across eastern New Mexico during the evening hours producing damaging wind gusts of 60 to 70 mph across parts of the east central plains. This complex of storms exited into Texas just before midnight. Daytime heating combined with an upper level disturbance moving into southern New Mexico resulted in the development of some strong thunderstorms across the higher terrain. Thunderstorms across the Sacramento Mountains dropped heavy rainfall in a short period of time on the South Fork and Salt burn scars around Ruidoso resulting in some flash flooding and debris flows in the Upper Canyon area just west of downtown Ruidoso. These storms combined into a larger complex of storms over Chaves County before exiting into West Texas during the early evening hours.||Key Impacts: Hail, Severe Thunderstorm Wind Gust, Flash Flooding, Debris Flow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The AWOS station at Cannon AFB measured a wind gust of 59 mph from nearby thunderstorms.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A backdoor front across northeast NM during the day progressed southwest to along the east slopes of the Sangre de Cristo Mountains during the evening hours. Strong upslope flow resulted in the quick development of a severe thunderstorm between Ute Park and Cimarron just before sunset. Nickel size hail was observed in the town of Cimarron with golf ball size hail in the core of the storm at Philmont Scout Ranch, a few miles south of Cimarron. This severe storm progressed east between NM 58 and NM 21 before gradually weakening and dissipating around Springer.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter at the St. James Hotel in Cimarron observed nickel size hail from a severe thunderstorm  tracking just south of the town.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper low moved across the desert southwest June 1st into June 2nd helping to pull up higher moisture from former Tropical Storm Alvin in the eastern Pacific. Widespread showers and storms developed across New Mexico during the day June 2nd as the upper low moved into the state. Storms resulted in severe wind gusts in Clovis, penny size hail and flash flooding in Albuquerque and Farmington. Flash flooding in the north diversion arroyo channel in east central Albuquerque resulted in a fatality. Storms across the state tapered off after sunset with the loss of daytime heating and the system exiting east of the state. A 2nd upper low approached the state on June 3rd resulting in the diurnal development of storms across the Four Corners area. Storms brought quarter size hail and flash flooding to the Farmington area. ||Another round of showers and storms developed across western NM on June 4th as the upper low entered the state. These storms moved east across the state bringing flash flooding and large hail to parts of the middle Rio Grande Valley and east central New Mexico during the afternoon and evening hours before exiting into the Texas Panhandle just before midnight. More rounds of severe storms developed along the dryline across far eastern New Mexico during the late afternoon and evening hours of June 5th and 6th. These storms brought large hail 1 to 2 inches in diameter to Curry and Roosevelt Counties both days and western Union County on June 6th. Additionally, several brief tornadoes were observed across east central and southeast Roosevelt County on June 5th.||Key Impacts: Hail, Flash Flood, Tornado, Severe Thunderstorm Wind Gust.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The AWOS at Cannon Air Force Base measured peak wind gusts of 59 mph from nearby thunderstorms.

Storms organized into a convective line across eastern New Mexico during the evening hours producing damaging wind gusts of 60 to 70 mph across parts of the east central plains. This complex of storms exited into Texas just before midnight. Daytime heating combined with an upper level disturbance moving into southern New Mexico resulted in the development of some strong thunderstorms across the higher terrain. Thunderstorms across the Sacramento Mountains dropped heavy rainfall in a short period of time on the South Fork and Salt burn scars around Ruidoso resulting in some flash flooding and debris flows in the Upper Canyon area just west of downtown Ruidoso. These storms combined into a larger complex of storms over Chaves County before exiting into West Texas during the early evening hours.||Key Impacts: Hail, Severe Thunderstorm Wind Gust, Flash Flooding, Debris Flow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter reported golf ball to baseball size hail from a severe thunderstorm. The hail resulted in tree, house and automobile damage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper low moved across the desert southwest June 1st into June 2nd helping to pull up higher moisture from former Tropical Storm Alvin in the eastern Pacific. Widespread showers and storms developed across New Mexico during the day June 2nd as the upper low moved into the state. Storms resulted in severe wind gusts in Clovis, penny size hail and flash flooding in Albuquerque and Farmington. Flash flooding in the north diversion arroyo channel in east central Albuquerque resulted in a fatality. Storms across the state tapered off after sunset with the loss of daytime heating and the system exiting east of the state. A 2nd upper low approached the state on June 3rd resulting in the diurnal development of storms across the Four Corners area. Storms brought quarter size hail and flash flooding to the Farmington area. ||Another round of showers and storms developed across western NM on June 4th as the upper low entered the state. These storms moved east across the state bringing flash flooding and large hail to parts of the middle Rio Grande Valley and east central New Mexico during the afternoon and evening hours before exiting into the Texas Panhandle just before midnight. More rounds of severe storms developed along the dryline across far eastern New Mexico during the late afternoon and evening hours of June 5th and 6th. These storms brought large hail 1 to 2 inches in diameter to Curry and Roosevelt Counties both days and western Union County on June 6th. Additionally, several brief tornadoes were observed across east central and southeast Roosevelt County on June 5th.||Key Impacts: Hail, Flash Flood, Tornado, Severe Thunderstorm Wind Gust.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter in northeast Farmington observed quarter size hail from a severe thunderstorm.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level trough crossing northern NM overlaid abundant moisture near the surface resulting in severe weather supercells developing along the east slopes of the Sangre de Cristo Mountains. These supercells produced severe hail ranging in size from quarters to hen egg size. Video of golf ball to hen egg size hail was shared from Philmont Scout Ranch in Colfax County, resulting in damage to vehicles, trees, and other structures. Thankfully there was no reports of injuries or fatalities. ||Key Words: Hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The public and a trained spotter reported nickel to quarter size hail from a severe thunderstorm passing over Springer, NM.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The monsoon high was centered across southeast Arizona and southwest New Mexico on August 1st and 2nd. Northwest flow around the upper high circulation along with embedded disturbances in this flow resulted in the development of showers and thunderstorms across parts of central and eastern New Mexico during the afternoon and evening hours. Thunderstorms resulted in some flash flooding on and downstream of the Ruidoso burn scars and in Lincoln on August 1st and on and downstream of the Salt burn scar on August 2nd. Meanwhile, severe storms across northeast and east central New Mexico resulted in some large hail near Ocate, Miami, Sedan, Hayden, and Amistad. Severe thunderstorms also produced damaging wind gusts in Santa Rosa and Hayden.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter and the county emergency manager reported water flowing over several roadways, including NM Highway 21 along with hail accumulating on the roads near Miami.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Abundant moisture pushed up to the east slopes of the central mountain chain of New Mexico. Combined with daytime heating, this initiated severe thunderstorms along the east slopes of the Sangre de Cristo Mountains southward along the central highlands. These storms progressed eastward over the eastern plains of NM toward the TX border producing severe hail ranging from nickels to half-dollar size. Flash flooding was also reported from locally heavy rainfall near Portales, and over the Blue 2 burn scar north of Ruidoso.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A traditional monsoon flow on the western flank of an upper level high over the central U.S. combined with daytime heating resulted in the development of shower and thunderstorm activity across the higher terrain during the early through late afternoon hours. Some of this shower and thunderstorm activity moved over the South Fork burn scar on both August 17th and August 18th resulting in flash flooding and debris flow across roadways on and downstream of the burn scar. Overall impacts were reported to be more severe and widespread on August 17th compared to August 18th. Finally, a brief landspout was observed in Las Vegas during the mid afternoon on August 18th as well as near the Bernalillo and Sandoval County line during the mid afternoon on August 19th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rainfall on the South Fork burn scar resulted in flash flooding and debris flow across roadways on and downstream of the burn scar. Debris flow was observed in multiple sections across Alpine Village Road, Cedar Creek Drive, Gavilan Canyon Road, Ash and Brady Canyon Road, White Mountain Drive at Mechem Drive and Geroge McCarthy Drive. This resulted in the closures of these roadways for a few hours in the mid to late afternoon. Finally, water in the Rio Ruidoso was observed overtopping the bridge at Gavilan Canyon Road just north of Sudderth Drive.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered virga showers over western and central NM developed along the Continental Divide during the afternoon of August 8th. These dry showers producing little to no rainfall spread into the Rio Grande Valley, producing dry downburst winds. One severe downburst wind impacted the Belen Regional Airport with a 61 mph wind gust. No damage was reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Belen Regional Airport measured a severe wind gust of 61 mph from a non-thunderstorm convective dry downburst from virga showers in the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: August 2025 saw well above normal temperatures and below normal precipitation across much of western and central NM with below normal temperatures and near to above average precipitation across eastern NM. The lack of precipitation across far western NM from this monsoon season resulted in the introduction of extreme drought across the West Central and Northwest Plateau and far Northwest Highlands.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Extreme drought conditions persisted across the Sandia Mountains portion of the zone.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The combination of long term drought and very dry atmospheric conditions alongside virga showers allowed the lightning start Middle Mesa Wildfire to quickly grow on August 2nd after being discovered the day before in an area east of Navajo Lake. The fire continued to grow to just over 5,000 acres by August 10th toward the east just south of the NM and CO border. Given the remote area of the wildfire, there were no damaged and destroyed structures. Only impacts along Forest Road 218A were noted.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Middle Mesa Wildfire was started by lightning just west of Navajo Lake and 22 miles west of Dulce, NM. It quickly grew out of control on August 2nd, reaching a total size of 5,045 acres by August 10th. Impacts were limited to very remote areas of far northwestern NM in the Carson National Forest along Forest Road 218A.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Vereda Blanca Wildfire was discovered August 8th in the Northeast Mt. Taylor Ranger District. Very dry conditions coupled with elevated winds allowed the fire to grow to 1,000 acres by August 10th, reaching a total size of 1,307 acres by August 13th. The fire was located northeast of Mt. Taylor along steep mesas right along the western border of Sandoval County. There were no reports of damages given the remote location of the fire.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Vereda Blanca Fire was a lightning start along a mesa northeast of Mt. Taylor in the Mt. Taylor Ranger District, located right along the western edge of Sandoval County north of Marquez. The fire grew to a total of 1,307 acres by August 13th. There have been no reports of damages given its remote location.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Monsoon Moisture across central and eastern New Mexico along with a backdoor front entering northeast NM resulted in the development of scattered shower and thunderstorm activity across these portions of the state. A slow moving thunderstorm resulted in flash flooding on State Highway 76 near Chimayo. Severe thunderstorms resulted in damaging wind gusts of up to 70 mph across Union and Harding County. Finally, mid to late evening thunderstorms resulted in some flash flooding at Bear Canyon near Ruidoso and in Portales.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rainfall from a slow moving thunderstorm resulted in rushing floodwaters across State Highway 76 near Mile Marker 8 in Chimayo.

Repeated round of thunderstorms resulted in flash flooding on the Ruidoso area burn scars, State Highway 117 in central Cibola County south of Grants and downstream of the Hermits Peak and Calf Canyon burn scar on State Highways 58 and 434 just north of Cleveland and Mora, respectively, in Mora County. Highway 434 in Mora County were closed down for a couple of days to allow for extensive repairs.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Slow moving thunderstorms resulted in flash flooding and rushing water across NM Highway 117 near El Malpais National Monument. The highway was temporarily closed during this time until flood waters fully receded.

Floodwaters covered State Road 2 and Jackson Road resulting in the temporary closure of these roadways in the area.||Daytime heating and lots of available moisture resulted in the new development of showers and thunderstorms across the higher terrain during the early afternoon hours. EVENT_NARRATIVE: State Road 2 was closed at the intersection with East Jackson Road due to flooding along the normally dry Walnut Arroyo. Based on photo provided, floodwaters looked to be about 1 foot deep and not moving.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An intense trough digging into the Four Corners region with rapid cyclogenesis over eastern Colorado produced high winds and blowing dust across much of the southern Great Plains.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A maximum wind gust of 58 MPH was observed at the CWOP site, GW0175 Lakewood.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Daytime heating combined with higher moisture east of a dryline and upper level northwest flow across the northern and central Rockies and Great Plains resulted in the development of severe storms with large hail across Colfax, Union and far northeast Harding Counties during the late afternoon and evening hours before exiting into West Texas after midnight. A more potent shortwave at the base of an upper low over the central U.S. and Canadian border moved across the central and southern Great Plains on June 8th resulting in the development of strong to severe storms across northeast and east central NM during the mid afternoon and evening hours until exiting into Texas at around sunset. Finally, a 61 mph wind gust was observed at Raton Crews Airport during the evening hours of June 11th due to a nearby thunderstorm.||These storms pushed a backdoor front and higher moisture south and west across eastern and central NM resulting in the development of another round of strong to severe storms across these parts of the state during the afternoon hours of June 9th. Storms organized into a convective line across eastern New Mexico during the evening hours producing damaging wind gusts of 60 to 70 mph across parts of the east central plains. This complex of storms exited into Texas just before midnight. Daytime heating combined with an upper level disturbance moving into southern New Mexico resulted in the development of some strong thunderstorms across the higher terrain. Thunderstorms across the Sacramento Mountains dropped heavy rainfall in a short period of time on the South Fork and Salt burn scars around Ruidoso resulting in some flash flooding and debris flows in the Upper Canyon area just west of downtown Ruidoso. These storms combined into a larger complex of storms over Chaves County before exiting into West Texas during the early evening hours.||Key Impacts: Hail, Severe Thunderstorm Wind Gust, Flash Flooding, Debris Flow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A storm chaser shared a video of estimated ping pong ball size hail along NM 453 just south of U.S. 64/87.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Intense daytime heating combined with higher midlevel moisture resulted in the development of virga showers and dry thunderstorms across the southwest mountains during the afternoon moving east into the lower Rio Grande Valley and south central highlands by the evening hours. This virga and dry thunderstorm activity resulted in a brief 61 mph wind gust at the Stallion Runway mesonet station and 63 mph wind gust at the Little Burro mesonet stations.||Key Words: Severe Thunderstorm Winds, Virga Showers, Dry Thunderstorms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The mesonet station at Stallion Runway on the White Sands Missile Range measured a 61 mph wind gust from nearby virga showers and dry thunderstorms.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The monsoon high was centered across southeast Arizona and southwest New Mexico on August 1st and 2nd. Northwest flow around the upper high circulation along with embedded disturbances in this flow resulted in the development of showers and thunderstorms across parts of central and eastern New Mexico during the afternoon and evening hours. Thunderstorms resulted in some flash flooding on and downstream of the Ruidoso burn scars and in Lincoln on August 1st and on and downstream of the Salt burn scar on August 2nd. Meanwhile, severe storms across northeast and east central New Mexico resulted in some large hail near Ocate, Miami, Sedan, Hayden, and Amistad. Severe thunderstorms also produced damaging wind gusts in Santa Rosa and Hayden.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A peak wind gust of 68 mph was measured by the AWOS at the Santa Rosa Route 66 Airport. This wind gust resulted in a large tree to be blown over in the downtown area.

Debris flow on Gavilan Canyon Road made the roadway impassible with a car containing an individual trapped in the debris. Thankfully, rescue crews were able to get the occupant out of the vehicle and debris flow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Higher moisture moved into eastern and central New Mexico behind a backdoor front resulting in a greater coverage of showers and thunderstorms across the middle Rio Grande Valley, central mountain chain, and northeast New Mexico. Repeated round of thunderstorms resulted in flash flooding on the Ruidoso area burn scars, State Highway 117 in central Cibola County south of Grants and downstream of the Hermits Peak and Calf Canyon burn scar on State Highways 58 and 434 just north of Cleveland and Mora, respectively, in Mora County. Highway 434 in Mora County were closed down for a couple of days to allow for extensive repairs.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Slow moving thunderstorms developed over the McBride burn scar back building east over the South Fork burn scar. Heavy rainfall rates first resulted in flash flooding on the McBride burn scar with debris flow resulting in the closure of Gavilan Canyon Road between Hull Road and Warrior Drive and Gavilan Canyon Road at Meander Drive. Debris flows and flash flooding then quickly occurred on the South Fork burn scar resulting in the closure of many roads including White Mountain Drive at Warren Tucker Drive, Alpine Village Road at University, Brady Canyon Road at Ponderosa Drive, Ash Drive, and Mechem Drive, Cree Meadows Drive, Sudderth Drive and Mechem Drive intersection, and Paradise Canyon Drive at Hull Road and Leon Farrar. The flash flooding and debris flows resulted in vehicles rushing down the road on Sunny Slope Drive and a water rescue of one male.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Higher moisture moved into eastern and central New Mexico behind a backdoor front resulting in a greater coverage of showers and thunderstorms across the middle Rio Grande Valley, central mountain chain, and northeast New Mexico. Highway 434 in Mora County were closed down for a couple of days to allow for extensive repairs.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Repeated rounds of thunderstorms resulted in flash Flooding along NM Highway 518 just north of Cleveland. A trained spotter observed at least 8 places where small creeks along the Rio Mora were exceeding banks and flooding the highway. Flash flooding also resulted in extreme damage to NM Highway 434. The highway was closed for emergency repairs for 2 to 3 days and was reopened a few days later after installing five corrugated metal pipes and restoring the roadway with a temporary dirt surface.

CZ_NAME: WEST SLOPES SACRAMENTO MOUNTAINS BELOW 7500 FEET
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A fast moving, negatively tilted trough moved from the Great Basin into New Mexico during the overnight hours of March 13-14.  Ahead of the trough and associated cold front, winds picked up dust over the Lordsburg Playa and El Paso.  As the cold front moved through overnight, winds gusted to 95 mph at San Augustin Pass with several other 70+ mph wind gusts noted.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A peak gust of 70 mph was recorded in High Rolls and the Mescalero RAWS recorded a gust of 68 mph. In addition to the wind gusts, a tree was downed near the tunnel on Highway 82 west of High Rolls.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A backdoor front across northeast NM during the day progressed southwest to along the east slopes of the Sangre de Cristo Mountains during the evening hours. Strong upslope flow resulted in the quick development of a severe thunderstorm between Ute Park and Cimarron just before sunset. Nickel size hail was observed in the town of Cimarron with golf ball size hail in the core of the storm at Philmont Scout Ranch, a few miles south of Cimarron. This severe storm progressed east between NM 58 and NM 21 before gradually weakening and dissipating around Springer.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Golf ball size hail was observed at Philmont Scout Ranch from a severe thunderstorm.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An early season plume of monsoon moisture advected northward into New Mexico from June 22nd through the 24th bringing several days of flash flooding and severe weather across portions of northern and central NM. June 22nd in particular saw severe thunderstorms produce numerous severe wind gusts of 60 mph or stronger across eastern NM from Roswell to Nara Visa with reports of downed utility poles and arcing powerlines near Roswell and dust being kicked up in Quay County. Heavy rains also produced flash flooding reports in Roswell where multiple cars and a fire truck were stuck in deep water. June 23rd saw thunderstorms bring burn scar flash flooding to the Ruidoso area burn scars where swift water rescues were performed at a mobile home park and a hotel. Flash flooding was also reported along an area where two to three inches or more of rain fell just south of I-40 in eastern NM near Santa Rosa and Newkirk. The Hermit's Peak/Calf Canyon burn scar saw flash flooding on June 24th from burn scar flash flooding when NM 518 was closed due to flood waters. Heavy rains also produced flash flooding in Belen, NM where debris and high waters washed out a portion of I-25 at mile marker 190 which required cleanup crews to reopen the next day. Monsoon moisture began to lower after the 24th as did the thunderstorm activity by June 25th. ||Key Impacts: Flash flooding, burn scar flash flooding, debris flow, thunderstorm wind gust.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A mesonet station near Newkirk measured a severe thunderstorm wind gust of 67 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper low moved across the desert southwest June 1st into June 2nd helping to pull up higher moisture from former Tropical Storm Alvin in the eastern Pacific. Widespread showers and storms developed across New Mexico during the day June 2nd as the upper low moved into the state. Storms resulted in severe wind gusts in Clovis, penny size hail and flash flooding in Albuquerque and Farmington. Flash flooding in the north diversion arroyo channel in east central Albuquerque resulted in a fatality. Storms across the state tapered off after sunset with the loss of daytime heating and the system exiting east of the state. A 2nd upper low approached the state on June 3rd resulting in the diurnal development of storms across the Four Corners area. Storms brought quarter size hail and flash flooding to the Farmington area. ||Another round of showers and storms developed across western NM on June 4th as the upper low entered the state. These storms moved east across the state bringing flash flooding and large hail to parts of the middle Rio Grande Valley and east central New Mexico during the afternoon and evening hours before exiting into the Texas Panhandle just before midnight. More rounds of severe storms developed along the dryline across far eastern New Mexico during the late afternoon and evening hours of June 5th and 6th. These storms brought large hail 1 to 2 inches in diameter to Curry and Roosevelt Counties both days and western Union County on June 6th. Additionally, several brief tornadoes were observed across east central and southeast Roosevelt County on June 5th.||Key Impacts: Hail, Flash Flood, Tornado, Severe Thunderstorm Wind Gust.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Valencia County emergency manager observed quarter size hail from a severe thunderstorm.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The setup today was similar to that of the June 5-6 event. Shortwave troughing, upslope flow, and a stationary boundary to the north allowed for scattered thunderstorm development in Southeast New Mexico and (to a lesser extent) in the Davis Mountains. A few storms became severe, producing large hail (especially in/around Hobbs) and damaging winds. These storms continued to produce severe weather as they tracked into the northern Permian Basin of West Texas, gradually dissipating overnight.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A report was received from the public of quarter-size hail southwest of Bledsoe. The time of occurrence is estimated from radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front/outflow boundary, coupled with vorticity maxima in the northwesterly flow aloft, resulted in the development of severe thunderstorms in the Permian Basin of West Texas and Southeast New Mexico during the evening and overnight hours on June 9th. Supercells formed initially, but storms eventually grew upscale later that night. These storms produced multiple instances of severe winds (especially the MCS) and large hail. A landspout was also reported in Martin County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Mesonet station NMC36 Malaga recorded a 67 mph wind gust. The station stopped reporting at 10:55 PM MDT as a line of storms neared, but when it came back online at 12:20 AM MDT (well after the line of storms had passed) it showed a 67 mph wind gust. As such, the time that the gust occurred is estimated from radar and from when the station first stopped reporting (which was likely caused by the severe wind gust).

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Outflow from thunderstorms pushed through Luna County and brought gusty winds and blowing dust reducing visibilities to near zero at times.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: New Mexico State Police reported near zero visibilities along I-10 east of Deming from outflow thunderstorm winds. The Deming ASOS also reported visibilities down to a quarter mile.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deep plume of early season monsoonal moisture moved up areas east of the Rio Grande Valley.  At the same time, an upper trough was entering the Great Basin helping to trigger numerous storms with heavy rain across the Rio Grande Valley of Dona Ana and Otero counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Workers on the White Sands Missile Range reported Flash Flooding near the intersection of Range Road Route 7 and 10.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Daytime heating combined with higher moisture east of a dryline and upper level northwest flow across the northern and central Rockies and Great Plains resulted in the development of severe storms with large hail across Colfax, Union and far northeast Harding Counties during the late afternoon and evening hours before exiting into West Texas after midnight. A more potent shortwave at the base of an upper low over the central U.S. and Canadian border moved across the central and southern Great Plains on June 8th resulting in the development of strong to severe storms across northeast and east central NM during the mid afternoon and evening hours until exiting into Texas at around sunset. Finally, a 61 mph wind gust was observed at Raton Crews Airport during the evening hours of June 11th due to a nearby thunderstorm.||These storms pushed a backdoor front and higher moisture south and west across eastern and central NM resulting in the development of another round of strong to severe storms across these parts of the state during the afternoon hours of June 9th. Storms organized into a convective line across eastern New Mexico during the evening hours producing damaging wind gusts of 60 to 70 mph across parts of the east central plains. This complex of storms exited into Texas just before midnight. Daytime heating combined with an upper level disturbance moving into southern New Mexico resulted in the development of some strong thunderstorms across the higher terrain. Thunderstorms across the Sacramento Mountains dropped heavy rainfall in a short period of time on the South Fork and Salt burn scars around Ruidoso resulting in some flash flooding and debris flows in the Upper Canyon area just west of downtown Ruidoso. These storms combined into a larger complex of storms over Chaves County before exiting into West Texas during the early evening hours.||Key Impacts: Hail, Severe Thunderstorm Wind Gust, Flash Flooding, Debris Flow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Ping pong to golf ball size hail was observed along NM 402 southwest of Sedan from a severe thunderstorm.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Intense daytime heating combined with a westward moving outflow boundary from a storm complex in Texas resulted in the development of strong to severe storms across the east slopes of the Sacramento Mountains during the early afternoon hours. These storms moved northeast across east central and southeast NM during the afternoon and evening hours bringing strong outflow wind gusts of up to 67 mph at the Roswell Air Center, large hail to trained spotters in western De Baca and northeast Roosevelt County, and a 70 mph wind gust to the mesonet station at Melrose Range. Additionally, nearby thunderstorms brought a 68 mph wind gust to Raton Crews Airport. Storms exited into West Texas just after sunset.||Key Words: Severe Thunderstorm Winds, Large Hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter along NM 294 south of Taiban observed a few golf ball sized hail among lots of pea size hail.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The setup today was similar to that of the June 5-6 event. Shortwave troughing, upslope flow, and a stationary boundary to the north allowed for scattered thunderstorm development in Southeast New Mexico and (to a lesser extent) in the Davis Mountains. A few storms became severe, producing large hail (especially in/around Hobbs) and damaging winds. These storms continued to produce severe weather as they tracked into the northern Permian Basin of West Texas, gradually dissipating overnight.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Emergency management sent reports of broken car windows and multiple alarm calls due to golf ball-size hail falling in Hobbs. The time of occurrence is estimated from radar. The cost of damages is also estimated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Shortwave troughing and upslope flow generated scattered thunderstorms in the higher terrain of West Texas. Meanwhile, troughing and a stationary front/outflow boundary helped generate thunderstorms across portions of Southeast New Mexico. A number of these storms became severe, producing damaging winds (including microbursts) and large hail. A landspout was reported near Loving, and flooding was also reported (especially in Pecos County).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: LANDSPOUT. Location: 32.350,-103.830/32.350,-103.835. A landspout was reported by the public via social media at the intersection of Wipp Road and NM 128. The beginning and ending locations of the landspout are radar estimated. The size of the landspout was approximately 25 yards and it lasted less than a minute.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An early season plume of monsoon moisture advected northward into New Mexico from June 22nd through the 24th bringing several days of flash flooding and severe weather across portions of northern and central NM. June 22nd in particular saw severe thunderstorms produce numerous severe wind gusts of 60 mph or stronger across eastern NM from Roswell to Nara Visa with reports of downed utility poles and arcing powerlines near Roswell and dust being kicked up in Quay County. Heavy rains also produced flash flooding reports in Roswell where multiple cars and a fire truck were stuck in deep water. June 23rd saw thunderstorms bring burn scar flash flooding to the Ruidoso area burn scars where swift water rescues were performed at a mobile home park and a hotel. Flash flooding was also reported along an area where two to three inches or more of rain fell just south of I-40 in eastern NM near Santa Rosa and Newkirk. The Hermit's Peak/Calf Canyon burn scar saw flash flooding on June 24th from burn scar flash flooding when NM 518 was closed due to flood waters. Heavy rains also produced flash flooding in Belen, NM where debris and high waters washed out a portion of I-25 at mile marker 190 which required cleanup crews to reopen the next day. Monsoon moisture began to lower after the 24th as did the thunderstorm activity by June 25th. ||Key Impacts: Flash flooding, burn scar flash flooding, debris flow, thunderstorm wind gust.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flash flooding off the Salt burn scar coming out of Bear Canyon. Swift water rescues  conducted at the Cherokee Mobile Home Park, the Elevate Hotel, and for three vehicles at the Otero Federal Credit Union. Flash flooding reported at the Twin Spruce RV park, U.S. Highway 70 and Sudderth Dr. and U.S. Highway 70 and mile mark 261 closed due to burn scar flash flooding. Damage is an estimate based on vehicles rescues. No injuries or fatalities reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Dry lightning strikes were observed during the middle portions of the week of June 11th, starting the Buck Fire in an extremely remote section of Catron County south of Pelona Mountain. Dry virga showers and a few isolated dry thunderstorms producing gusty and erratic winds allowed the fire to rapidly grow Friday June 13th and through the weekend from roughly 550 acres to over 33,000 acres by Sunday June 15th and to a final size of 57,736 acres by June 17th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Buck Fire was discovered in an extremely remote area of Catron County near Pelona Mountain southwest of Magdalena, NM. The fire burned mainly through wilderness area on private land along and northwest of highway 163 reaching 57,736 acres by June 17th. No damage and destruction of property was reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Amidst afternoon thunderstorm activity, outflow gusts surging through the Tijeras Canyon produced severe wind gusts that blew up abundant amounts of dust in the southern Albuquerque metro area. This dust produced an area of low visibility.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A convectively induced outflow gust that funneled through the Tijeras Canyon reached a peak gust of 66 mph. No lightning was observed at the Sunport.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An incoming trough, upslope flow, and a somewhat weak dryline resulted in the development of scattered thunderstorms across Southeast New Mexico and West Texas. A few of these storms became severe, producing damaging winds and large hail. The two most notable storms occurred in Fort Stockton and Pleasant Farms. Microbursts in these locations resulted in winds in excess of 100 mph and widespread damage to roofs and mobile homes. Two people were injured in Pleasant Farms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Power lines were reported down at mile markers 53-55 on Highway 128 east of Jal. The time of occurrence is estimated by radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper low moved across the desert southwest June 1st into June 2nd helping to pull up higher moisture from former Tropical Storm Alvin in the eastern Pacific. Widespread showers and storms developed across New Mexico during the day June 2nd as the upper low moved into the state. Storms resulted in severe wind gusts in Clovis, penny size hail and flash flooding in Albuquerque and Farmington. Flash flooding in the north diversion arroyo channel in east central Albuquerque resulted in a fatality. Storms across the state tapered off after sunset with the loss of daytime heating and the system exiting east of the state. A 2nd upper low approached the state on June 3rd resulting in the diurnal development of storms across the Four Corners area. Storms brought quarter size hail and flash flooding to the Farmington area. ||Another round of showers and storms developed across western NM on June 4th as the upper low entered the state. These storms moved east across the state bringing flash flooding and large hail to parts of the middle Rio Grande Valley and east central New Mexico during the afternoon and evening hours before exiting into the Texas Panhandle just before midnight. More rounds of severe storms developed along the dryline across far eastern New Mexico during the late afternoon and evening hours of June 5th and 6th. These storms brought large hail 1 to 2 inches in diameter to Curry and Roosevelt Counties both days and western Union County on June 6th. Additionally, several brief tornadoes were observed across east central and southeast Roosevelt County on June 5th.||Key Impacts: Hail, Flash Flood, Tornado, Severe Thunderstorm Wind Gust.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Video shared on social media by local media showed severe flash flooding and flowing water filled with debris across a property at Calle De Baca and Highway 116 in southwest Belen. A large work trailer was swept away in the flood waters. The flash flooding plugged one culvert and wiped out a second culvert. Additionally, the heavy rainfall resulted in overflowing retention ponds resulting in localized flooding for other nearby residents.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An early season plume of monsoon moisture advected northward into New Mexico from June 22nd through the 24th bringing several days of flash flooding and severe weather across portions of northern and central NM. June 22nd in particular saw severe thunderstorms produce numerous severe wind gusts of 60 mph or stronger across eastern NM from Roswell to Nara Visa with reports of downed utility poles and arcing powerlines near Roswell and dust being kicked up in Quay County. Heavy rains also produced flash flooding reports in Roswell where multiple cars and a fire truck were stuck in deep water. June 23rd saw thunderstorms bring burn scar flash flooding to the Ruidoso area burn scars where swift water rescues were performed at a mobile home park and a hotel. Flash flooding was also reported along an area where two to three inches or more of rain fell just south of I-40 in eastern NM near Santa Rosa and Newkirk. The Hermit's Peak/Calf Canyon burn scar saw flash flooding on June 24th from burn scar flash flooding when NM 518 was closed due to flood waters. Heavy rains also produced flash flooding in Belen, NM where debris and high waters washed out a portion of I-25 at mile marker 190 which required cleanup crews to reopen the next day. Monsoon moisture began to lower after the 24th as did the thunderstorm activity by June 25th. ||Key Impacts: Flash flooding, burn scar flash flooding, debris flow, thunderstorm wind gust.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The public reported a severe thunderstorm wind gust of 60 mph near San Jon, NM.
