EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Daytime heating combined with higher moisture east of a dryline and upper level northwest flow across the northern and central Rockies and Great Plains resulted in the development of severe storms with large hail across Colfax, Union and far northeast Harding Counties during the late afternoon and evening hours before exiting into West Texas after midnight. A more potent shortwave at the base of an upper low over the central U.S. and Canadian border moved across the central and southern Great Plains on June 8th resulting in the development of strong to severe storms across northeast and east central NM during the mid afternoon and evening hours until exiting into Texas at around sunset. Finally, a 61 mph wind gust was observed at Raton Crews Airport during the evening hours of June 11th due to a nearby thunderstorm.||These storms pushed a backdoor front and higher moisture south and west across eastern and central NM resulting in the development of another round of strong to severe storms across these parts of the state during the afternoon hours of June 9th. Storms organized into a convective line across eastern New Mexico during the evening hours producing damaging wind gusts of 60 to 70 mph across parts of the east central plains. This complex of storms exited into Texas just before midnight. Daytime heating combined with an upper level disturbance moving into southern New Mexico resulted in the development of some strong thunderstorms across the higher terrain. Thunderstorms across the Sacramento Mountains dropped heavy rainfall in a short period of time on the South Fork and Salt burn scars around Ruidoso resulting in some flash flooding and debris flows in the Upper Canyon area just west of downtown Ruidoso. These storms combined into a larger complex of storms over Chaves County before exiting into West Texas during the early evening hours.||Key Impacts: Hail, Severe Thunderstorm Wind Gust, Flash Flooding, Debris Flow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain from thunderstorms over the South Fork and Blue 2 burn scar resulted in flash flooding and debris flows of 6 to 8 inches on North Loop, Main Road, and Brady Canyon Road in the Upper Canyon area just west of downtown Ruidoso. Some flowing water and debris flow was also observed on Ski Run Road and Highway 48 near the Alto Reservoir.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level storm system initially brought a mix of valley rain and mountain snow to northern and central NM. The first precipitation reports entered northwestern NM with the arrival of the initial upper level trough, spreading and expanding across the northern mountains and adjacent highlands southward to the I-40 corridor between Albuquerque and Santa Rosa by late Wednesday December 3rd. Mesoscale snowbands began to setup as a surface cold front advanced south and west across eastern NM and upslope into the east slopes of the central mountain chain Wednesday evening. This enhanced snow fall through the East Mountain Communities east of Albuquerque and the Sandia Mountains where 6 to 12 inches of snow was reported by Thursday morning on December 4th. An unfortunate semi-truck accident occurred along I-40 just east of Clines Corners at roughly 4:30am MST on the morning of December 4th. Slick and icy roads caused the driver to lose control and have their semi-truck rollover while another semi-truck crashed into them. Two other trucks were involved with one catching fire and becoming engulfed in flames according to a KOB news media report of the incident. Snow cleared out just before sunrise on the morning of December 4th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Local news station KOB reported a deadly semi-truck crash along I-40 just east of Clines Corners. The semi-truck lost control and rolled over with another semi-truck crashing into it, with two other trucks crashing into them. The first semi-truck is likely the one that caught fire and became engulfed in flames. There was unfortunately one fatality reported while another was taken to the hospital. Damage is an estimate of the four crashed trucks.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A dry upper level disturbance moved southeast across the state during the morning hours of December 6th. Strong mid-level northwest winds in the wake of this disturbance resulted in a brief high wind gust of 60 mph at a mesonet station southeast of Vaughn during the late morning hours. Northwest winds tapered off during the afternoon hours as the disturbance exited east into the Great Plains.

A traditional monsoon flow on the western flank of an upper level high over the central U.S. combined with daytime heating resulted in the development of shower and thunderstorm activity across the higher terrain during the early through late afternoon hours. Some of this shower and thunderstorm activity moved over the South Fork burn scar on both August 17th and August 18th resulting in flash flooding and debris flow across roadways on and downstream of the burn scar. Overall impacts were reported to be more severe and widespread on August 17th compared to August 18th. Finally, a brief landspout was observed in Las Vegas during the mid afternoon on August 18th as well as near the Bernalillo and Sandoval County line during the mid afternoon on August 19th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A video shared by broadcast media shows a landspout near the Bernalillo/Sandoval County line. Location and timing are estimated based on the information provided from broadcast media.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Remnants of two tropical systems from the eastern Pacific moved over the Desert Southwest in the early part of the month providing above normal precipitation over the Four Corners and northwestern portion of New Mexico. This provided modest improvements to drought conditions over portions of western NM. Meanwhile, below normal precipitation was observed across eastern NM where drought conditions remained consistent for much of this half of the state with the exception of some degradation through southeastern NM.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Extreme drought persisted across much of the zone.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level disturbance moved across the northern and central Rockies during the early morning hours of October 20th. Strong west winds at the base of this disturbance resulted in some mountain wave activity across the Sangre de Cristo Mountains. This mountain wave activity produced a 60 mph wind gust along I-25 at Rowe as well as tree and powerline damage near Mineral Hill. These strong west-northwest winds across the Sangre de Cristo Mountains and nearby highlands tapered off during the midday hours as the disturbance exited into the northern and central Great Plains.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The mesonet station along I-25 at Rowe measured a brief wind gust of 60 mph, likely from a crashing mountain wave.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A backdoor front moved into eastern NM on October 6th. This front along with southeast return flow resulted in some isolated strong to severe storms across east central NM during the evening hours with one storm producing nickel size hail near Puerto de Luna. The front and higher moisture behind it pushed through the gaps of the central mountain chain into central New Mexico late October 6th into early October 7th. This backdoor front along with the higher moisture in place set the stage for the development of severe thunderstorms across the middle Rio Grande Valley and south central highlands during the late afternoon through mid evening hours on October 7th. These storms produced large hail across far northern Socorro County along with flash flooding across Interstate 25 and State Highway 116 in Bernardo. Storms moved into east central and southeast New Mexico during the overnight hours and morning hours of October 8th resulting in some flash flooding near the Rio Felix south of Roswell.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Law enforcement observed quarter size hail from a severe thunderstorm on State Highway 169 at mile marker 28.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Subtropical moisture associated with the remnants of Hurricane Priscilla in the eastern Pacific moved across northwest New Mexico on October 10th resulting in scattered shower and thunderstorm activity across this part of the state. Thunderstorms resulted in flash flooding in the normally dry Estes Arroyo northwest of Aztec. These floodwaters washed away a vehicle driving through the arroyo at County Road 3078. Thankfully, the occupant of the vehicle was rescued by a county fire and rescue crew and sustained no injuries.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A vehicle was washed away in fast moving water several feet deep in the normally dry Estes Arroyo at County Road 3078. The single occupant in the vehicle was rescued without sustaining any injuries.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the evening of the 28th/early morning hours of the 29th, a strong cold front pushed through west Texas and southeast New Mexico, bringing Arctic air back to the region. Meanwhile, our area ended up under the right entrance region of an upper-level jet. A narrow band of wintery precipitation formed from roughly Queen, New Mexico into the Permian Basin of west Texas towards Midland/Odessa. Snow eventually became the dominant precipitation type within this narrow band. The most snow fell in southeast New Mexico, particularly in Carlsbad, where up to 9.8 inches fell. Further east into west Texas towards Midland/Odessa, an inch was commonly reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A member of the public commented on a trained storm spotter's Facebook page reporting 3 inches of snow on the ground in Queen in the Guadalupe Mountains. The total time of occurrence is partially estimated by radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level storm system initially brought a mix of valley rain and mountain snow to northern and central NM. The first precipitation reports entered northwestern NM with the arrival of the initial upper level trough, spreading and expanding across the northern mountains and adjacent highlands southward to the I-40 corridor between Albuquerque and Santa Rosa by late Wednesday December 3rd. Mesoscale snowbands began to setup as a surface cold front advanced south and west across eastern NM and upslope into the east slopes of the central mountain chain Wednesday evening. This enhanced snow fall through the East Mountain Communities east of Albuquerque and the Sandia Mountains where 6 to 12 inches of snow was reported by Thursday morning on December 4th. An unfortunate semi-truck accident occurred along I-40 just east of Clines Corners at roughly 4:30am MST on the morning of December 4th. Slick and icy roads caused the driver to lose control and have their semi-truck rollover while another semi-truck crashed into them. Two other trucks were involved with one catching fire and becoming engulfed in flames according to a KOB news media report of the incident. Snow cleared out just before sunrise on the morning of December 4th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A CoCoRaHS report of 9 inches along I-25 near Rowe, NM. There was 2 to 5 inches of snow elsewhere across the zone.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong portion of the polar jetstream crossed just north of New Mexico with strong winds stretching southward over northern NM during the evening of December 17th and lasting overnight well into the morning of December 18th. High wind gusts of 58 to 65 mph were observed in areas along and east of the Sangre de Cristo Mountains down the I-25 corridor. Strong winds just below high wind criteria were also observed elsewhere across the central highlands and eastern plains of New Mexico, including a 57 mph wind gust at Clines Corners along I-40.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A public weather station near Gallinas, NM measured a peak wind gust of 65 mph. Another station in Rainsville also measured high sustained winds of 48 mph.

Northwest winds across the central highlands tapered off during the evening hours as the disturbance exited into the Great Plains.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: November saw a mixed bag of above normal and below normal precipitation across the state, heavily defined by one notable winter system late in the month. With November's climatological precipitation amounts already fairly low, it didn't take much for that one storm to bring above normal precipitation to a southwest to northeast swath across the state leaving the northwest and southeast corners with below normal precipitation. Modest one category improvements were observed over portions of west-central NM alongside some one category degradations for parts of the Sangre de Cristo Mountains and southeastern plains. Most of the state saw little to no change in their drought status.

CZ_NAME: MIDDLE RIO GRANDE VALLEY/ALBUQUERQUE METRO AREA
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: September 2025 saw near to slightly below normal temperatures across northern and eastern New Mexico and slightly above normal temperatures across western and central New Mexico. Above normal precipitation was observed across western and central New Mexico with near to slightly below normal precipitation across far eastern New Mexico. The above normal precipitation across central New Mexico resulted in the removal of severe drought conditions across the Tusas Mountains, Jemez Mountains, and Rio Grande Valley. However, extreme drought persisted across far western New Mexico.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Extreme drought conditions were removed from the zone.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A troughing pattern from the Pacific brought in a late season monsoon burst of moisture to the Desert Southwest. Strong to severe thunderstorms developed over portions of the state with more locally heavy rainfall events occurring from the abundant monsoon moisture. Flash flooding was observed in several locations ranging from near El Malpais south of Grants, NM, in Navajo Nation west of Crownpoint, and in Tijeras east of Albuquerque. One woman was reportedly swept away from flash flooding south of Gallup near Red Rock.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Reports on social media and by the NM DOT showed pictures of flash flooding over NM Highway 117 between mile markers 44 and 51.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Monsoon moisture returned to southern, eastern, and much of central New Mexico on September 16th. This higher moisture combined with disturbances rotating around the southern periphery of an upper low over the northern and central Rocky Mountains resulted in the development of showers and thunderstorms during the midday hours across these portions of the state. A thunderstorm produced a wet microburst over the Las Vegas Municipal Airport during the mid-afternoon hours resulting in an 81 mph wind gust at the site. Additionally, slow moving thunderstorms on I-25 at Algodones produced flash flooding on the highway and railroad, resulting in backups and delays for several hours during the evening. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A troughing pattern from the Pacific brought in a late season monsoon burst of moisture to the Desert Southwest. Strong to severe thunderstorms developed over portions of the state with more locally heavy rainfall events occurring from the abundant monsoon moisture. Flash flooding was observed in several locations ranging from near El Malpais south of Grants, NM, in Navajo Nation west of Crownpoint, and in Tijeras east of Albuquerque. One woman was reportedly swept away from flash flooding south of Gallup near Red Rock.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Quarter size hail reported by a former NWS Employee.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rainfall from slow moving thunderstorms resulted in water and debris from nearby drainages covering portions of I-25 between mile markers 248 and 249 near Algodones. The interstate was reduced to 1 lane with traffic backed up 4 miles in both directions for several hours during the evening. The Rail Runner tracks at mile marker 14 on Pan American Central Highway in Algodones was also flooded with debris over the railroad tracks and a bridge being washed out. This resulted in a 3 hour delay to allow for repairs to the railroad track and bridge. Northbound travelers were bussed from the stop on Highway 550 to the San Felipe station to continue their trip, while southbound travelers were bussed from the stop to the Highway 550 station.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A nearby trough, coupled with a very moist airmass, led to the development of a line of showers and thunderstorms in New Mexico during the morning of the 13th. This line moved across Southeast New Mexico and West Texas throughout the day, strengthening in the afternoon thanks to diurnal destabilization. Additional thunderstorms developed in Southeast New Mexico behind this line in the higher terrain. Though no severe wind/hail was reported, multiple reports of flash flooding were received, mainly from Lamesa and Artesia. Activity tended to wane after dark.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flash Flooding was reported along US 285 and Main St in Artesia by an NWS employee via a member of the public. The total time of occurrence is partially estimated by radar. No damage was reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Higher moisture lingered across southern New Mexico on September 18th. This higher moisture along with a backdoor front along the east slopes of the Sacramento Mountains resulted in shower and thunderstorm development across the mountain range and burn scars surrounding Ruidoso during the midday hours. Thunderstorms over the South Fork burn scar intensified during the early afternoon hours resulting in flash flooding and debris flows on and downstream of the burn scar. Impacts were observed as far east as the Ruidoso Downs racetrack. Storms across the Sacramento Mountains tapered off during the mid-to-late afternoon hours with floodwaters and debris flows gradually receding during this time as well.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain from thunderstorms over the South Fork burn scar produced water and debris in Cedar Creek flowing over the bridge at Brady Canyon Road and Ponderosa Drive before moving across Mechem Drive at Brady Canyon Road, shutting down Mechem Drive. Water and debris in Cedar Creek continued downstream moving over the road at Cree Meadows and Country Club Drive and Paradise Canyon Drive at Starlite Road and Hull Road, closing these roads. Video from social media showed floodwaters and debris flows swiftly going around a house along the banks of the Rio Ruidoso at the first bridge on Main Road in the Upper Canyon neighborhood. The Rio Ruidoso was observed flowing over the bridge at Country Club Drive and Eagle Drive resulting in the closure of these two roadways. Finally, the floodwater and debris flow in the Rio Ruidoso overwhelmed culverts under the western part of the Ruidoso Downs racetrack eventually moving across the racetrack itself.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Higher moisture moved into New Mexico between an upper low off the coast of southern California and an upper high over northern Mexico. This higher moisture combined with an upper level disturbance moving quickly east along the southern end of an upper low over the northern and central Rockies resulting in the development of showers and thunderstorms across northern and central New Mexico. Storms across south central New Mexico resulted in an estimated 60 mph wind gust in Magdelena and a peak wind gust of 62 mph at the Phillips Hull mesonet on the White Sands Missile Range. Thunderstorms coalesced into a squall line as they moved across eastern NM during the early to mid-evening hours. Peak wind gusts of 69 mph at Melrose Range and 64 mph at the Roswell Air Center and a West Texas Mesonet near Endee in eastern Quay County were measured along the leading edge of this squall line. This line of showers and thunderstorms moved east into West Texas shortly thereafter.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter in Magdalena reported an estimated peak wind gust of about 60 mph from a severe thunderstorm.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A trough to the west, coupled with the effects of moist upslope flow, helped trigger scattered to numerous showers and thunderstorms across far West Texas and Southeast New Mexico throughout the day on September 28th. While no severe weather was reported, ample moisture and terrain effects resulted in at least one instance of flash flooding in the Davis Mountains and one instance of flooding due to runoff near Carlsbad.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Eddy County Office of Emergency Management reported flooding from higher terrain runoff on West Loop Road between Hidalgo Street and Lea Street. The total time of occurrence is estimated. No damage was reported.

Strong winds at the leading edge of a line of thunderstorms moving into the Albuquerque Metro resulted in a 58 mph wind gust on the West Mesa of Albuquerque.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Social media video showed evidence of mud flows and damage from flash flooding near the town of Monticello.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A negatively tilted trough was moving through southern California and northern Baja which tapped plenty of subtropical moisture.  Locally heavy rain developed along the east slopes of the Blacks Range and brought flash flooding and wind damage to the Monticello area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Social media report showed snapped power poles around Monticello as well as a damaged barn.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A late season monsoonal moisture push to the Rio Grande combined with an upper trough moving northeast through the Four Corners region brought severe thunderstorms to parts of Otero County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Apache Mesonet site recorded a peak gust of 58 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper trough was moving through the Great Basin region with some weak convergence setup over the Rio Grande Valley which contributed to severe thunderstorm development and flash flooding. Storms also developed over the Gila region which brought heavy rain to the Trout burn scar resulting in some flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Report of a water rescue for a vehicle being washed into an arroyo.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A nearby trough, coupled with a very moist airmass, led to the development of a line of showers and thunderstorms in New Mexico during the morning of the 13th. This line moved across Southeast New Mexico and West Texas throughout the day, strengthening in the afternoon thanks to diurnal destabilization. Additional thunderstorms developed in Southeast New Mexico behind this line in the higher terrain. Though no severe wind/hail was reported, multiple reports of flash flooding were received, mainly from Lamesa and Artesia. Activity tended to wane after dark.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Bolton Rd closed at Eagle Draw by Eddy County Emergency Management due to flash  flooding. The total time of occurrence is partially estimated by radar. No damage was reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the evening of the 28th/early morning hours of the 29th, a strong cold front pushed through west Texas and southeast New Mexico, bringing Arctic air back to the region. Meanwhile, our area ended up under the right entrance region of an upper-level jet. A narrow band of wintery precipitation formed from roughly Queen, New Mexico into the Permian Basin of west Texas towards Midland/Odessa. Snow eventually became the dominant precipitation type within this narrow band. The most snow fell in southeast New Mexico, particularly in Carlsbad, where up to 9.8 inches fell. Further east into west Texas towards Midland/Odessa, an inch was commonly reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Law enforcement reported 1 to 2 inches of snowfall throughout the day in Eunice. The time of occurrence is partially estimated by radar.

According to the spotter's updates, Winter Storm criteria was achieved around 8:30am MST. The total time of snowfall occurrence is partially estimated from radar and the last of the spotter's reports.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: September 2025 saw near to slightly below normal temperatures across northern and eastern New Mexico and slightly above normal temperatures across western and central New Mexico. Above normal precipitation was observed across western and central New Mexico with near to slightly below normal precipitation across far eastern New Mexico. The above normal precipitation across central New Mexico resulted in the removal of severe drought conditions across the Tusas Mountains, Jemez Mountains, and Rio Grande Valley. However, extreme drought persisted across far western New Mexico.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Extreme drought expanded north to the far southwest portion of the zone.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A surge in monsoon moisture from the remnants of Hurricane Lorena in the eastern Pacific combined with disturbances embedded in the northwest flow aloft resulting in a higher coverage of showers and thunderstorms across northern and central NM. Thunderstorms during the evening hours on September 5th resulted in flash flooding across the Pojoaque Pueblo between Santa Fe and Espanola.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rainfall from thunderstorms resulted in Dry Creek Road to County Road 84 near the Rio Pojoaque to become impassable due to water over the roadways. Additionally, the intersection at the end of NM 502 and Cities of Gold Road in Pojoaque became impassible due to 4 feet of water over the roadways.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak disturbance rotating southeast around a ridge of high pressure over the Desert Southwest combined with higher moisture along and east of the central mountain chain resulting in the development of isolated strong to severe storms along the east slopes of the Sangre de Cristo Mountains. One of these storms produced nickel size hail at Ojo Feliz in Mora County. Isolated thunderstorms across eastern New Mexico quickly dissipated during the evening hours around sunset due to the loss of daytime heating.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter in Ojo Feliz observed nickel size hail from a strong thunderstorm.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak disturbance rounded a ridge centered over the Rocky Mountains and Desert Southwest on September 9th. Along with increasing surface moisture, this resulted in the development of scattered showers and thunderstorms across central and eastern NM. One thunderstorm produced a severe wind gust of 63 mph at Tucumcari Municipal Airport as it moved through the site. Shower and thunderstorm activity slowly tapered off across central New Mexico overnight.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 63 mph wind gust was observed at Tucumcari Municipal Airport as a severe thunderstorm moved through the site.
